O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. Learned counsel appearing on behalf of the Revenue fairly
states that the issues involved in this batch of matters is
squarely  covered by our order dated 10.04.2026 passed in C.A.
No.4716/2026  and  connected  matters,  read  with  order  dated
04.05.2026 passed in C.A. No.6922/2026 and connected matters.
4. The appeals are, thus, disposed of in the same terms. The
matters are remitted to the jurisdictional High Courts to be
decided in light of the above-cited order(s) of this Court.
5. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.

       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
    (JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
..........................J.
       (V. MOHANA)

JULY 14, 2026

          O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant-husband and the respondent No.1-wife
have been able to reach to an amicable settlement. The
Settlement Agreement dated 25th May, 2026 has been reduced
into  writing  duly  signed  by  the  parties  and  their
respective counsel including the learned Mediator.
4. The Settlement Agreement reads thus:
“SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT
This Settlement Agreement is entered into on this 25 th day of May,
2026 at New Delhi.
BY AND BETWEEN
Ms. Tanvi Sinha ,  @ Tanvi  D/o Dr. Shivendra Kumar Sinha, R/o
Deepa  Girls  PG,  Matawali  Gali,  Sukhrali,  Sector  17  Gurugram
Haryana  -122011,  Permanent  address  G-2,  Ganpati  Apartments
Saraidhela, beside Citi bazar, VPO Dhanbad Jharkhand, hereinafter
referred  as  “Tanvi/First  Party” (which  expression  shall,  unless
1

--- Page 37 ---
repugnant to the context or meaning thereof, be deemed to mean and
include  its  nominees,  representatives,  successors,  assignees,
administrators, etc.)
AND
Mr. Abhineet, S/o Sh. Binod Kumar Shrivastava R/o  Flat No.
F-404,  BPTP  Park  Serene,  Sector-37  D,  Gurgaon-122001,
hereinafter  referred  as  “Abhineet/Second  Party”  (which
expression shall, unless repugnant to the context or meaning
thereof,  be  deemed  to  mean  and  include  its  nominees,
representatives, successors, assignees, administrators, etc.)
(Hereinafter collectively referred to as ‘Parties’)
                 WHEREAS:
A. The Parties were husband and wife, who got married to
each other on 22 nd November, 2019 at Lemon Tree Hotel
Sector 60, Gurugram, Haryana, according to the Hindu rites
and customs.
B. After  the  marriage  both  the  Parties  lived  together  as
husband  and  wife  and  marriage  was  duly  consummated.
However, there is no issue from this wedlock. 
C. However,  due  to  temperamental  differences,
incompatibility,  misunderstandings  and  matrimonial
discord, disputes and differences arose between the parties
shortly after the marriage, as a consequence whereof the
parties started residing separately and have not cohabited
since  03.01.2022  and  several  complaints,  litigations  and
criminal proceedings came to be initiated by and against
each other and their respective family members. There is no
possibility  of the  Parties  living  together in  the  future  as
2

--- Page 38 ---
husband and wife.
D. Vide  order  dated  15.05.2026  passed  by  the  Hon’ble
Supreme Court of India in SLP (Diary) No. 12289/2026, the
parties were referred to Supreme Court Mediation Centre
for mediation and pursuant  to the discussions  inter se  the
Parties and with the assistance of the Mediator, the Parties
have now decided to resolve their disputes and have decided
to reduce in writing the various terms and conditions on
which  they  have  settled  their  disputes  and  differences
amicably.  The  Parties  have  amicably  settled  all  their
disputes,  grudges  and  grievances  against  each  other  and
against each other’s family members as per the terms of the
present Agreement, subject to the fulfilment of the terms
and conditions of the present Agreement. The Parties have
agreed to bind themselves to the terms detailed hereinunder.
Accordingly, the Parties have decided to execute the present
Agreement,  and with the assistance of the Mediator have
voluntarily,  without  any  force,  or  coercion with  the
following agreements, conditions and terms which are set
out herein under:
3

--- Page 39 ---
THIS     AGREEMENT     THEREFORE     WITNESSETH     AS     UNDER:      
1) The  First  Party  confirms  and  acknowledges  that  all  her
belongings  and  possessions  including  clothes,  jewellery,
stridhan,  etc.  are  in  her  possession  and  that  the  present
Mediated Agreement is, inter-alia, a full and final settlement
regarding all her claims (past, present and future) towards
stridhan, maintenance, alimony (temporary or permanent),
any movable or immovable properties, etc. and as such she
is, henceforth left with no claim whatsoever in this regard
from the Second Party and/or his family. 
2) That  both  Parties  are  well-qualified  and  well-educated
individuals and, with a view to put a quietus to all their past,
present and future disputes and litigations once and for all,
the First Party hereby agrees, confirms, acknowledges and
undertakes that she shall not raise, assert, pursue or stake
any  claim,  whether  past,  present  or  future,  against  the
Second Party towards maintenance, stridhan, permanent or
temporary  alimony,  or  in  respect  of  any  movable  or
immovable  property,  etc.,  in  any  manner  whatsoever,
pursuant  to  the  execution  of  the  present  Settlement
Agreement. 
3) That subject to the fulfilment of all the terms and conditions
of  the  present  Settlement Agreement  /  Memorandum  of
Understanding, the Parties herein agree and undertake that
they  have  No  Objection  to  the  quashing  of  all  pending
litigations, including any / all criminal and civil proceedings,
instituted by any of the Parties against each other and / or

4) That it is agreed and undertaken by parties that after signing
of the present Settlement Agreement, the parties herein shall
file an appropriate Joint Application / Joint Petition under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India before the Hon’ble
Supreme Court of India seeking amicable dissolution of their

necessary statements before the Hon’ble Supreme Court or

furnished by the Second Party in CHI/38270/2022 emanating
from FIR No. 98/2022 and undertakes to make an appropriate
statement to that effect before the concerned Court(s), if so
required.
7) That apart from the legal proceedings specifically mentioned
hereinabove which are required to be withdrawn / quashed, it
is agreed by and between the Parties that all / any other civil,
criminal or quasi-criminal proceedings, complaints, petitions,
or cases of any nature whatsoever, whether pending before
any  Court,  police  authority,  tribunal,  or  any  other  forum,
which may have been initiated by either Party against the
other or at the behest of either Party, shall be deemed to have
been  fully  and  finally  compromised,  settled  and  resolved
inter se . The Parties further agree and undertake to take all
necessary steps for the withdrawal and / or quashing of such
proceedings, if any, and undertake not to pursue or initiate
any further action in relation thereto in the future. It is agreed
and  undertaken  by  the  Parties  that  this  shall  include  the
withdrawal of Complaints / representations / grievance raised
or made against any of the counsels representing either of the
parties in any of the proceedings.

and complying with the terms of the present Agreement in its

members, relatives, friends, etc., either under Civil Law or
Criminal Law within the jurisdiction in India or abroad and
all the cases lodged by the Parties shall stand withdrawn /
quashed subject to fulfilment of all the terms of the present
Agreement.
9) The parties agree and undertake that they shall have no claim
or right qua any movable or immovable property of each other
or  their  family  members  whether  self-acquired,  HUF  or
ancestral, under any circumstances, now or in future subject to
fulfilment of terms and conditions of the present Settlement
Agreement.
10) That subject to the fulfilment of the present Agreement, the
Parties  agree  and  undertake  that  they  are  giving  up  any
grievance and they shall not have any grievances against each
other,  therefore,  in  view  of  the  same,  they  have  further
undertaken that they shall not level any allegations or file any
cases  against  each  other  or  each  other’s  parents/family/
relatives and shall not act in a manner so as to  harm the
reputation and image of each other, in the family or at their
work place or the society at large in future.

Agreement/MOU,  the  Parties  and  their  respective  family
members, relatives, representatives and persons, etc. shall be

--- Page 46 ---

members/relatives, shall hereafter initiate, institute, pursue,
support or cause to be filed any complaint, petition, case,

Second Party had gifted one mobile phone, namely Realme 7,

undertake that the said mobile phone is and shall be treated as
the  exclusive  property  of  the  First  Party,  she  being  in

fastened upon or construed against the Second Party or his
family members.
14) That the First Party hereby expressly agrees, confirms and
undertakes that she shall not use, represent, disclose, continue

the  execution  of  the  present  Settlement  Agreement.  It  is

or consequence arising out of the past, present or future use

Party nor any of his family members shall in any manner
whatsoever  be  liable,  responsible  or  accountable  for  the

--- Page 48 ---
same.

16) That the Parties agree and undertake that they have executed
the  present  Settlement  Agreement  /  MOU  without

17) That in case any of the Parties fails to comply with any of the

SHAMSHER    … APPELLANT
VERSUS
THE STATE OF CHHATTISGARH    … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The  present  appeal  arises  out  of  the  final
judgment  and  order  dated  05.03.2013  (Impugned
Judgement) passed by the High Court of Chhattisgarh
at  Bilaspur  (High  Court)  in  Criminal  Appeal  No.
348/2003.  The  High  Court,  vide the  Impugned
Judgement, upheld the appellant’s conviction under
Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and
Sections 25/27 of the Arms Act, 1959, and confirmed
the sentence of life imprisonment imposed upon him.
However,  while  doing  so,  the  co-accused,  i.e.
Yashwant  Nagwanshi  (Accused  No.  2)  and  Mohandas
Manikpuri (Accused No. 3), were acquitted of the
charges  framed  under  Section  302/34  of  the  IPC.
Page 1 of 13

--- Page 55 ---
Instead, their conviction was modified to one under
Section 323 of the IPC, and they were sentenced to
one year of rigorous imprisonment. Since they had
already served the specified period and were already
on bail, their bail bonds were continued for six
months thereafter.
2. To understand the substance of the challenge
raised, we must first advert to the background of the
case. Arvind Kumar (Complainant/PW-4) lodged FIR No.
74/2001 on 06.08.2001 against the appellant under
Section 302 of the IPC and under Sections 25/27 of
the Arms Act, 1959, and against the two co-accused
under Sections 302/34 of the IPC, for the murder of
Shatrughan @ Gudda (deceased).
3. As per the prosecution case, the deceased, the
Complainant,  Chintu  @  Prashant  Kumar  (PW-3),
Roopendra Kumar (PW-8), and the three accused were
close friends. At around 6:00 pm on 06.08.2001, the
deceased, the Complainant, Chintu, and Mahendra met
at  Roopendra  Kumar’s  house.  Shortly  thereafter,
Accused No. 2 also came and informed them that the
Page 2 of 13

--- Page 56 ---
appellant had a dispute with the deceased and was
planning  to  assault  him.  He  suggested  that  they
accompany  him  to  the  house  of  the  appellant  to
amicably  resolve  the  matter.  Thereafter,  the
Complainant, Mahendra, and Chintu proceeded to the
house of the appellant alongwith the deceased and
Accused  No.  2,  where  Accused  No.  3  was  already
present. 
4. It was claimed that, as soon as they arrived,
Accused No. 2 lifted the deceased and knocked him
down. Then, Accused No. 2 and 3 dashed the deceased
on the floor and began assaulting him. In the midst
of the scuffle, the appellant took out a knife, which
he had concealed behind his back, and stabbed the
deceased twice, due to which the deceased started
bleeding.  Alarmed,  the  Complainant  called  his
brother,  who  then  informed  the  police.  In  the
meantime, the three accused persons fled from the
scene, while the deceased was taken to the hospital. 
5. Once the police arrived at the scene of the
occurrence, they promptly recorded the Dehati Nalishi
Page 3 of 13

--- Page 57 ---
(Zero  FIR)  based  on  the  statement  given  by  the
Complainant  and  thereafter,  FIR  No.  74/2001  was
registered. It is pertinent to note that the Dehati
Nalishi records the entire case of the prosecution as
well as the names of the three accused persons.
6. At the hospital, the deceased was examined by Dr.
H. Shah (PW-13), who found two stab wounds: (i) a
deep stab wound measuring 4 cm x ½ cm on the left
side of the chest; and (ii) a stab wound measuring 3
cm x ½ cm on the right thigh. When the police arrived
at the hospital, in the opinion of Dr. H. Shah, the
deceased was unfit to give any statement. Thereafter,
despite medical treatment, the deceased succumbed to
his injuries and passed away at about 7:45 pm on the
same day. As per the postmortem report, which was
prepared by Dr. S. K. Fating (PW-16), the cause of
death was shock on account of haemorrhage as a result
of the above-mentioned injuries, and the death was
homicidal in nature. 
7. After  the  accused  persons  were  taken  into
custody,  an  iron-made  skewer-shaped  knife  with  a
Page 4 of 13

--- Page 58 ---
handle, measuring 13 inches, was recovered at the
instance  of  the  appellant.  Certain  blood-stained
clothes and other articles were also seized at the
behest of the co-accused.
8. At  this  juncture,  it  is  necessary  to  note
particular aspects of the depositions given by the
relevant  eyewitnesses  before  the  7 th Additional
Sessions Judge (FTC), Durg (Trial Court) in Sessions
Trial No. 327/2001. In this regard, Chintu deposed
that two days prior to the incident, the deceased had
beaten up the appellant at the latter’s house. Chintu
further deposed that on the day of the incident,
Accused No. 2 approached him and suggested that a
compromise be effected between the deceased and the
appellant. It was only thereafter that the incident
in  question  took  place,  which  resulted  in  the
deceased’s demise. His testimony was corroborated by
the Complainant. The injuries mentioned during the
deposition are also in consonance with the postmortem
report.
Page 5 of 13

--- Page 59 ---
9. Roopendra Kumar, notably, did not fully support
the  prosecution  case  and  was  declared  hostile.
However,  he  corroborated  part  of  the  other
eyewitnesses’ statements regarding the quarrel that
took place between the appellant and the deceased two
days prior to the incident. He also confirmed that
all three accused had hit the deceased with their
fists. 
10. Pashupatinath Singh (PW-7), Roopendra Kumar, and
Jagadish Ram (PW-12/father of the deceased) deposed
that on the night before the incident, the three
accused as well as four other persons came to the
deceased’s house, enquired about his whereabouts, and
threatened to kill him.  
11. Dr. H. Shah was also examined during the trial,
where he categorically stated that when the deceased
was brought to him at the hospital, he was not in a
condition to speak. He further deposed that though
the sizes of the two wounds on the deceased’s body
were  different,  there  was  a  possibility  of  both
wounds being caused by any sharp and hard object. The
Page 6 of 13

--- Page 60 ---
testimonies of Dr. H. Shah and Dr. S. K. Fating prove
that  the  deceased  died  as  a  result  of  grievous
antemortem injuries, which caused severe damage and
lacerations to his internal organs. 
12. The accused examined Dharmendra as a defence
witness, who deposed that the deceased was talking
for about one hour while at the hospital.
13. The Trial Court, on a detailed examination of the
facts and evidence, concluded that the prosecution
had  succeeded  in  proving,  beyond  all  reasonable
doubt, that the death of the deceased was homicidal
in nature and was caused by the accused persons in
pursuance of a common intention. Consequently, they
were all sentenced to imprisonment for life.
14. Aggrieved by his conviction, the appellant filed
Criminal Appeal No.348/2003, while the two co-accused
filed Criminal Appeal No. 342/2003 before the High
Court. In his appeal, the appellant argued that the
evidence  of  eyewitnesses  was  inconsistent  and,
therefore, the conviction based on their testimonies
Page 7 of 13

--- Page 61 ---
ought not to be sustained. In the alternative, it was
contended that since the appellant had dealt only one
blow to the deceased, it could not be said that he
had the intention to commit murder. Thus, he ought to
be convicted of a lesser offence. The two co-accused
also  argued  that  they  did  not  share  a  common
intention with the appellant and, therefore, their
convictions could not be sustained.  
15. Upon  detailed  consideration,  the  High  Court
upheld the conviction and sentence imposed by the
Trial Court as far as the appellant was concerned.
However, the High Court set aside the conviction of
the two co-accused under Section 302/34 of the IPC as
they had no prior knowledge that the appellant was
carrying a weapon during the assault. They came to
know of the same only when the appellant pulled out
the knife and attacked the deceased. As a result, it
could not be held that the two co-accused intended to
commit  murder  of  the  deceased  or  cause  grievous
injury to him. Thus, the overt act leading to the
deceased’s death was an individual act attributable
Page 8 of 13

--- Page 62 ---
to the appellant, and the co-accused could only be
convicted of the lesser offence under Section 323 of
the IPC for assaulting the deceased with their fists.
16. Assailing  his  concurrent  conviction,  the
appellant has approached this Court.
17. We  have  heard  the  learned  counsel  for  the
parties. The counsel for the appellant submitted that
the offence took place in the midst of a sudden brawl
and  was  caused  by  the  heat  of  the  moment.  He
contended that since the offence was not perpetrated
with the intention of causing death or with the
knowledge that death would be caused, it could not be
classified as murder under Section 300 of the IPC,
and therefore, a lesser sentence ought to be imposed
as against him. Additionally, it was contended that
the evidence of the prosecution witnesses was shaky
and the testimony of the defence witness ought to be
taken as a dying declaration.
18. It  is  clear  that  the  eyewitnesses  have
consistently deposed to the incident. After examining
Page 9 of 13

--- Page 63 ---
Dr. H. Shah’s testimony, the time of the incident as
recorded in the FIR, and the time of death, all in
conjunction  with  the  testimony  of  the  defence
witness-Dharmendra,  it  is  evident  that  when  the
deceased was brought to the hospital, he was not in a
condition to speak. The time of the incident is 6:20
pm, and the death occurred at 7:45 pm. In these
circumstances, the testimony of the defence witness
that the deceased was talking for one hour after
being brought to the hospital and was able to give a
statement does not inspire confidence. Further, the
doctor  clearly  recorded  that  he  was  not  in  a
condition to speak when he was admitted. It is also
to be noted that there was no mention of such a
statement even in the cross-examination of PWs 3, 4,
7, and 12. Hence, the appellant’s argument that the
statement of the deceased ought to have been recorded
must be rejected.
19. In  the  light  of  the  above  facts  and
circumstances, the background of the incident, and in
view of the conduct on the part of the appellant,
Page 10 of 13

--- Page 64 ---
though it could be held that he had the intention to
cause injury to the deceased, the other attending
aspect which may be relevant is that he had no
knowledge that the injury would result in the death
of  the  deceased.  Considering  the  nature  of  the
evidence and the fact that it was a fight amongst
friends, we are of the view that the Criminal Appeal
needs to be allowed in part so as to modify the
conviction of the appellant from one under Section
302 of the IPC to one under Section 304(1) of the
IPC. Consequently, the sentence is reduced to the
period already undergone by the appellant. Since the
appellant is already on bail, his bail bonds stand
discharged. 
20. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed in part.
 …………………………………,CJI.
  [SURYA KANT]
 ………………………………………,J.
    [V. MOHANA]
Page 11 of 13

--- Page 65 ---
NEW DELHI;
JUNE 18, 2026

Diary No(s). 59790/2025 (XII)

321490/2025 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE

                             O R D E R
1

--- Page 76 ---
1. The present Special Leave Petition is arising out of
the order dated 10.02.2026, passed by the High Court of
Allahabad, by which the High Court has granted Interim Bail
to the petitioner for a period of three months.
2. It has been brought to the notice of this Court that
the order granting interim bail has been further extended
till 07.08.2026.
3. This Court has carefully gone through the order
granting interim bail for three months and no cogent reason
has been assigned while granting interim bail and the
interim bail has already been extended for a period of
three months. Resultantly, the High Court is requested to
decide the bail petition finally, instead of granting
interim bail in the matter.
4. The exercise of passing appropriate final order be
concluded as early as possibly, preferably within a period
of two months from today.
5. The Trial Court shall also be free to decide the
matter finally at an early date.
6. The observation made by the High Court in the order
granting interim bail, will not come in the way of the
Trial Court while deciding the matter finally, on merits.
7. With  the  above  observation,  the  Special  Leave
Petition stands disposed of.
2

--- Page 77 ---
8. Pending applications(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

                   Mr. Ankit Goel, AOR
                   Ms. Indira Bhakar, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
I.A. No.192333/2026
1. This application has been moved by the State of Uttar Pradesh,
2

--- Page 83 ---
Department of Infrastructure and Industrial Development seeking
impleadment in the underlying matter. During the course of the
hearing, certain clarification on direction no.8(viii) of our order
dated 13.08.2025 is also sought. The said direction reads as
follows:
“8(viii). As regard to the prioritization of Environmental
Impact Assessments, the Principal Secretary, Department of
Environment, Forest and Climate Change, State of U.P. and
the Chief Executive Officer, NOIDA are directed to ensure
that  no  project  is  given  effect  without  having
Environmental Impact Assessments and approval of such
report by the Green Bench of this Court.”
2. The application is founded upon the Government of India
notification dated 14.09.2006, a copy of which has been appended as
Annexure  A-5  with  the  application,  where  the  building  and
construction projects of an area less than 20,000 sq. mts. are
exempted from the precondition of environmental clearance. It is
clarified that the direction contained in paragraph 8 (viii) above
applies to such cases where environmental clearance based upon the
Environmental Impact Assessment Report is mandatorily required. It
goes without saying that for projects which have been exempted from
the requirement of prior environmental clearance under clause 8(a)
of the notification dated 14.09.2006, the Prescribed Authority
shall follow the conditions as may be required under the Government
notification/rules.
3. The Interlocutory Application is, accordingly, disposed of.
SLP(Crl.) No(s).1251/2023 & SLP(Crl) No.2808/2023
4. These proceedings were initially initiated seeking pre-arrest
bail in Case Crime No. 581/2021 registered at Police Station Noida,
3

--- Page 84 ---
Sector 20, Gautam Buddha Nagar, Uttar Pradesh. The allegations were
regarding the alleged release of a huge amount of compensation in
favour of some landowners, who were stated to be not entitled to
such higher compensation in lieu of their acquired land. Keeping in
mind that the outcome of the criminal proceedings were likely to
have a large scale impact on landowners whose lands were acquired
from time to time, the scope of these proceedings was enlarged and
it was deemed appropriate to direct a thorough investigation into
the conduct of the various categories of officers of Noida,
especially as to whether the due diligence was observed while
releasing the compensation purported in compliance of the Court
orders. Consequently, a comprehensive order was first passed on
23.01.2025, followed by the order dated 13.08.2025, under which a
special investigation team was constituted and various directions,
as contained in paragraph 8 of the later order, were issued. 
5. Periodical status reports have been filed by SIT, finally
inter alia pointing out that six FIRs have been registered in the
matter. We may hasten to add that vide the above-stated order dated
13.08.2025, the SIT was constituted by including some officers, who
were holding different positions, not necessarily in the State
Vigilance Bureau. After conducting a preliminary inquiry, the SIT
registered FIRs against several middlemen and government officials
who allegedly extorted money from farmers on the pretext of
facilitating the release of or securing an enhancement in their
land compensation. Some of these officers/officials have been
specifically named in the FIRs bearing nos.263, 264, 265, 266, 267
and 268 of 2026.
4

--- Page 85 ---
6. Ms. Ruchira Goel, learned State counsel, on instructions,
submits that since FIRs have now been registered by SIT, their
investigation,  having  regard  to  the  allegations  of
corruption/economic  offences,  be  entrusted  to  the  Vigilance
Department of the State. 
7. We find some substance in the plea taken on behalf of the
State as the subject FIRs are now required to be investigated by a
team of experts in investigating economic offences. 
8. Consequently, the SIT constituted by this Court is absolved of
its further responsibility to investigate the matters. However, the
State of Uttar Pradesh, through the Department of Home, is directed
to entrust the investigation of the above-mentioned FIRs to the
Vigilance Department of the State, which will take over the
investigation forthwith. The entire record of the SIT shall be
entrusted to the Vigilance Department of the State. An endeavour
shall be made to complete the investigation as early as possible
but not later than three months. The necessary consequences shall
follow, keeping the outcome of the investigation.
9. We clarify that the previous orders or this order have not
expressed any opinion on the merits of the allegations. The
jurisdictional Courts shall, therefore, be at liberty to form their
opinion in accordance with law.
10. Since the matter pertains to the perceived misconduct or
laxity on the part of the government officials/officers of the
Government Authorities, liberty is granted to any public-spirited
person to approach the High Court or this Court in case there is an
inordinate delay either in completion of the investigation or in
5

--- Page 86 ---
consequential prosecution/further action, as may be required under
the law.
11. The  directions  issued  earlier  re: protection  of  farmers
against arrest shall continue in force.
12. The Special Leave Petitions are, accordingly, disposed of.
13. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
(ARJUN BISHT)                              (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
6

--- Page 87 ---

…RESPONDENT(S)
                                      O R D E R
1. The matter comes upon for consideration on the referred
Miscellaneous Application along with Office Report dated 4 th
April 2026 and 10th July 2026.
2. Vide judgment dated 18th December 2025 in Civil Appeal
Nos. 14875 – 14880 of 2025, along with connected matters,
this Court directed the Appellant-Students therein, who were
to be benefitted by the judgment, to file an affidavit in terms
of  the  directions  given  in  para  no.  57.  As  per  the  Office
1

--- Page 88 ---
Report, in compliance thereof, a total of 259 affidavits have
been  filed  before  the  Registrar  (Judicial),  Rajasthan  High
Court, Jodhpur. Out of these 259 affidavits, 228 affidavits (Sl.
No. 1 to 228) have been filed within the stipulated period of 8
weeks,  i.e.  on  or  before  12 th February  2026,  whereas
remaining affidavits (Sl. No. 229 to 259) have been filed after
12th February 2026. 
3. The  original  affidavits  received  from  the  Appellant-
Students therein (Sl. No. 1 to 232) were forwarded to the
Principal  Secretary,  Medical  Education  Department,
Government of Rajasthan, vide forwarding letter dated 25 th
February  2026,  and  copies  thereof  were  forwarded  to  the
Rajasthan University of Health Sciences, vide letter dated 9 th
March  2026  by  the  Registrar  (Judicial),  Rajasthan  High
Court, Jodhpur. Thereafter, 27 more original affidavits have
been received from the other Appellant-Students therein. 
4. As per office report those 27 original affidavits (Sl. No.
233  to  259)  were  forwarded  to  this  Court  by  the
Registrar(Judicial), Rajasthan High Court, Jodhpur, instead
2

--- Page 89 ---
of forwarding it to the Principal Secretary, Medical Education
Department,  Government  of  Rajasthan.  In  such
circumstances, it is directed that those 27 original affidavits
received by this Court, vide letter dated 25 th April 2026, be
returned to the Registrar (Judicial), Rajasthan High Court,
Jodhpur, with a direction to forward the same to the Principal
Secretary,  Medical  Education  Department,  Government  of
Rajasthan,  as  well  as  Rajasthan  University  of  Health
Sciences. 
5. It is further noticed that in para no. 62 of the judgment
dated 18 th December 2025, the Division Bench directed the
Appellant-Colleges therein, to deposit a cost of Rs. 10 Crores
each and the State of Rajasthan to deposit a sum of Rs. 10
Lakhs  with  the  Rajasthan  State  Legal  Services  Authority
within a period of 8 weeks from the date of pronouncement of
judgment. The said 8 weeks expired on 12th February 2026. 
6. The  Registry  of  this  Court  is  directed  to  collect  the
information  from  the  Registrar  (Judicial),  Rajasthan  High
Court, Jodhpur, as to whether the Appellant-Colleges therein,
3

--- Page 90 ---
and the State of Rajasthan have complied with the directions
contained  in  para  no.  62  of  the  judgment  dated  18 th
December 2025.

Non-Reportable
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No.                     of 2026
(@ Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No.8495 of 2026)
Kamaljit Kaur & Anr.                           ...Appellants
Versus
The State of Punjab         ...Respondent
O R D E R
Leave granted.  
2. This  appeal  arises  from  the  Judgment  and  Order
dated 29.04.2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab and
Haryana at Chandigarh in CRM-M-20779 of 2026 whereby
the High Court dismissed the prayer for Anticipatory Bail to
the  appellants  in  FIR  No.  163  dated  02.04.2026,  PS
Zirakpur, Distt. SAS Nagar, Mohali, Punjab. 
3. On  11.05.2026,  when  the  matter  was  posted,  Mr.
Avadh Bihari Kaushik, learned AOR appeared for the 3rd
party (Sanjeev Kumar) and waived service of notice to him.
Mr. APS Deol, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the
appellants made a statement that his clients are ready and
willing to refund the entire amount received by him from
the 1st informant.  The  de facto  complainant, Mr. Sanjiv
Crl. Appeal @ SLP Crl. No.8495 of 2026                                                                           Page 1 of 6

--- Page 100 ---
Kumar s/o Shri Lakhmi Chand, resident of House No.1031,
Sector 12-A, Panchkula, Haryana, since appearing through
counsel, is impleaded as the second respondent who is
represented by Mr. Avadh Bihari Kaushik, learned AoR.   
 
4. T oday when the matter was taken up, the learned
Senior Counsel referred to the additional documents filed;
specifically,  the  three  Demand  Drafts  taken  out  in  the
name  of  the  de-facto  complainant  totaling
Rs.1,14,45,000/- which includes the earnest money deposit
of  Rs.1,05,00,000/-  and  interest  of  Rs.9,45,000/-.  The
learned  Counsel  appearing  for  the  3rd  party  de-facto
complainant,  however,  insisted  that  he  should  be  left
remedy to approach the civil court upon which we gave
him  the  option  of  either  approaching  the  civil  court  or
accepting the refund of the advance paid with interest, to
bring quietus to the matter. The learned Counsel took time
to  seek  instructions  from  his  client,  who  is  said  to  be
present  before  court  in  person.  After  consultation,  the
learned  Counsel  specifically  informed  us  that  his  client
would approach the civil court for specific performance.
5. Obviously, the issue agitated is a civil dispute which
has been sought to be converted to a criminal prosecution.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP Crl. No.8495 of 2026                                                                           Page 2 of 6

--- Page 101 ---
The very allegation in the criminal case is that an earnest
money  deposit  of  Rs.1,05,00,000/-  was  paid  to  the
appellants herein as advance for sale of land measuring 2
Bighas situated at village Gazipur, for a total consideration
of  Rs.3,75,000/-.  Criminal  liability  was  urged  on  the
contention that the execution of the sale deed was being
delayed and there were threats levelled of creating 3rd
party interests.  As we noticed, the matter is a civil dispute
which is to be agitated before a civil court. We are also
persuaded by the bona fides displayed by the appellants
who  voluntarily  undertook  to  pay  the  entire  amounts
together with interest in accordance with the undertaking
made before this Court; which the 3rd party is not inclined
to  accept.  There  cannot  be  found  any  culpability  in  a
criminal offence.  
6. The application is one for anticipatory bail, but we are
of the opinion that permitting continuance of the criminal
proceedings would be an abuse of process of law. Hence in
the peculiar circumstances, we are persuaded to quash
the proceedings and there shall be no further steps taken
under  FIR No.  163 dated  02.04.2026 at Zirakpur  Police
Station. However, we make it clear that the 3rd party who
Crl. Appeal @ SLP Crl. No.8495 of 2026                                                                           Page 3 of 6

--- Page 102 ---
has appeared before this Court and impleaded as the 2nd
respondent herein, is left liberty to pursue civil remedies
subject to laws of limitation. We also make it clear that if
at all the 2nd respondent’s claim for specific performance is
declined and an alternative plea is made of refund, he
cannot  claim  any  interest  on  the  same  since  he  has
declined the offer of refund with interest before us. We
make  it  clear  that  we  have  not  made  any  observation
regarding the merits in a claim for specific performance or
refund of earnest money deposit but only injuncted the
claim of interest, if eventually refund is contemplated. 
  
7. With  the  above  reservation  and  observations,  the
appeal stands allowed.
8. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
……....……………………. J.
                                         (J. B. PARDIWALA)
...…….……………………. J.
                                               (K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI
JULY 14, 2026.
    
Crl. Appeal @ SLP Crl. No.8495 of 2026                                                                           Page 4 of 6

--- Page 105 ---
1
    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
  CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No.________ OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(Civil) No.35928/2025)
   MADHABI DAS                                  Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
  ORIENTAL INSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED & ANR.     Respondent(s)
       O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant/ claimant preferred
Claim Petition upon death of the deceased
victim, Dipak Das, aged 25 years who died
in  a  road  accident  which  occurred  on
24.06.2019. The deceased was a vegetable
vendor. His notional income was assessed
by the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (for
short, MACT’) at Rs.5,000/- per month and
the same has been increased by the High
Court,  under  the  impugned  order,  at
Rs.6,000/- per month.
3. Referring to the judgment rendered
by this Court in Civil Appeal No. 2138 of

--- Page 106 ---
2
2026 in the case of  Smt. Sarathi Barman
versus Reliance General Insurance Company
Ltd. and Anr. vide order dated 18.02.2026,
it is argued that the appellant is also
entitled to compensation of Rs.14,00,000/-
for the reason that in the matter of Smt.
Sarathi Barman (supra)  also, the deceased
was a vegetable vendor.
4. Learned  counsel  for  the
respondent(s) submits that the deceased
can be treated as an unskilled person
therefore, on the basis of the Minimum
Wage Notification issued by the Government
of West Bengal on 24.06.2019 which was in
operation on the date of the accident, the
Minimum  Wage  for  an  unskilled  person
belonging  to  Zone-B  category  was
Rs.7176.00/-. Therefore, appellant’s claim
for  notional  income  at  Rs.9,000/-  per
month is not acceptable.
5. It is also submitted that in the
matter of  Smt. Sarathi Barman (supra) ,
this Court has fixed lumpsum compensation
without laying down any principle of law.

--- Page 107 ---
3
Therefore,  the  said  judgment  is  not
applicable.
6. Considering  the  order  passed  by
this Court in Smt. Sarathi Barman (supra),
wherein also, the deceased was a vegetable
vendor, we deem it appropriate to allow
the  appeal  in  part  by  enhancing  the
compensation  to  a  lumpsum  amount  of
Rs,14,00,000/-.
7. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed
in part and the amount awarded by the
MACT,  as  modified  by  the  High  Court,
stands further modified directing that the
appellant/ claimant shall be entitled to a
compensation  of  Rs.14,00,000/-  with
interest at the same rate as has been
awarded by the MACT.
8. The  respondent-Insurance  Company
shall be liable to pay the balance amount
as,  according  to  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the Insurance Company, the
amount  awarded  by  the  High  Court  has
already  been  paid  to  the
appellant/claimant. Let the balance amount

--- Page 108 ---
4
be  deposited  by  the  Insurance  Company
directly  to  the  bank  account  of  the
appellant/claimant  within  a  period  of
three months, details of which shall be
given by the appellant/claimant to the
Insurance Company.
9. Pending  application(s),  if  any,
shall stand closed.
......................J.
 (PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA)
.....................J.
       (SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR)
New Delhi
13-07-2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SLP (CRL)
NO.10652/2026]
ANISH @ NILESH                    
Appellant(s)
VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH            
Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  is  seeking
suspension of sentence during the
pendency of his appeal before the
High  Court  challenging  his
conviction for the offences under
Sections 302/34, 504 and 506 of
the  Indian  Penal  Code,  1860
arising out of FIR No.153 of 2017
dated  08.07.2017,  registered  at
SLP (CRL) NO.10652/2026  1/4

--- Page 118 ---
Police  Station  Kurra,  District-
Mainpuri.
We have heard the learned
counsel appearing for the parties.
Learned  counsel  appearing
for the appellant submitted that
apart from the ground of parity
with  the  other  co-accused,  the
appellant  has  already  undergone
around 10 years of incarceration
with remission. 
Learned  counsel  appearing
for the State submitted that, on
the last occasion when the appeal
was listed for hearing before the
High  Court,  the  appellant
requested for a pass over. 
The fact remains that the
appellant has completed 10 years
SLP (CRL) NO.10652/2026  2/4

--- Page 119 ---
of incarceration with remission.
The  appellant  did  not  seek  an
adjournment but only requested for
a pass over before the High Court.
We also want to take note
of the fact that the co-accused
though  standing  in  a  slightly
different  footing,  have  already
been  granted  suspension  of
sentence. We do feel that for the
appropriate conduct of the appeal,
the appellant is required to be
released especially when he has
been  in  incarceration  for  a
considerable amount of time. 
Considering the above, we
are  inclined  to  set  aside  the
impugned  order  and  suspend  the
sentence of the appellant.  Thus,
SLP (CRL) NO.10652/2026  3/4

--- Page 120 ---
the  sentence  imposed  upon  the
appellant stands suspended during
the pendency of his appeal before
the High Court and the appellant
is to be released on bail on terms
and conditions to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed,
accordingly.
Pending application(s), if
any, shall stand disposed of.
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026.
SLP (CRL) NO.10652/2026  4/4

Delay condoned.
We  have  heard  the  learned  Additional  Solicitor

there is no application of Section 31 or Section 427 of
5

--- Page 128 ---
the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973.

respondent(s) submits that though on the facts of the
case, the said submission is correct, one has to see that
the High Court has the power of discretion and that the
exercise  of  such  discretion  does  not  need  any
interference.

General  and  the  learned  Senior  counsel,  we  are  not
inclined  to  interfere  with  the  impugned  order(s)
particularly when several years have elapsed since then.

considerable  length  of  time.  Thus,  it  will  only  be
appropriate to request the High Court to expedite the
hearing  of  the  appeals  and  to  dispose  of  the  same,
preferably within a period of six months from today.

accordingly.  We make it clear that all the legal issues
are left open.

disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.  15847/2025
HITESH KUMAR ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR. ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant is an accused in FIR No.0106
dated  07.06.2021  registered  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 406 and 420 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 (“IPC”) and subsequently,
Sections 467, 468, 471 and 120-B of the IPC  came
to be added.
4. The complainant – respondent No.2 alleged
that appellant alongwith others pretending to be
the agents of PNB MetLife Insurance sold her a
fake insurance policy purportedly worth for Rs.
12,40,000/-  with  a  forged  receipt  showing
Rs.15,00,000/-.  It  is  alleged  that  upon
1

--- Page 130 ---
verification, she discovered that the accused
persons were neither the employees nor the agents
of the Insurance Company and as such she sought
for  action  being  taken  against  the  accused
persons by filing a complaint. On the basis of
said complaint, the aforementioned FIR came to be
registered and the application for anticipatory
bail filed by the appellant before the trial
court and the High Court came to be dismissed.
Hence, this appeal.
5. During the course of the pendency of the
present proceedings, the parties are said to have
entered  into  a  settlement  under  which  the
complainant, i.e., respondent No.2 herein is said
to have received the amounts due to her and she
has also submitted before the Court by appearing
virtually and reiterated that she has received
the said amount and there are no amounts due to
her from accused persons. In view of the same and
also in view of the statement made across the Bar
that a petition for quashing of the criminal
2

--- Page 131 ---
proceedings  would  be  filed  before  the
jurisdictional High Court, we see no impediment
for the appellant being enlarged on anticipatory
bail. Hence, the appeal is allowed. The appellant
is ordered to be released on anticipatory bail by
the jurisdictional Investigating Officer in view
of the settlement having been arrived at and in
the event of proceedings not being quashed, the
appellant should undertake to appear before the
Investigating  Officer  as  well  as  the
jurisdictional Court as and when called upon and
also on the hearing dates before the courts
below. With these observations, the appeal stands
allowed.
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stands
consigned to records.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 14, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NOS.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NOS.11290-
11292/2026]
BHAGAJI                              APPELLANT(S)
                      VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR.      RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. The  appellant’s  prayer  for  suspension  of
sentence awarded by the Trial Court pending appeal
before the High Court has been rejected thrice by
the High Court.
4. The submission on behalf of the appellant is
that initially appellant’s son was implicated.
However, when the DNA report could not confirm
that the child was related to appellant’s son, the
victim  took  a  somersault  and  implicated  the
appellant.  It is submitted that subsequently the
DNA of the child got matched. However, it is the
case of the appellant that the authenticity of the
DNA report is under challenge in the appeal.
Besides, the appellant is a 70 years’ old man and
is visually blind. It has also been submitted that
he has suffered incarceration for a period close
to three years. 
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.11290-11292/2026                                                           1

--- Page 135 ---
5. In these circumstances, while issuing notice
on  09.06.2026,  this  Court  had  released  the
appellant  on  interim  bail.   The  order  dated
09.06.2026 is extracted below:
“1. Delay condoned. 
2. Issue notice.
3.  Learned  counsel  has  appeared  for
respondent no. 1-State of Maharashtra on
advance notice. As such, no formal notice
be sent to the respondent no. 1. Notice
be only issued to respondent no. 2.
4. In the meantime, counter affidavit be
filed by learned counsel appearing for
respondent  no.  1-State.  SLP  (Crl.)  @
Diary No. 31972/2026.
5.  Considering  the  age  and  health
condition of the petitioner, who is aged
about 71 years, we are inclined to direct
petitioner’s release on interim bail.
6.  Accordingly,  the  petitioner  is
directed to be released on interim bail
in connection with FIR No. 0243 of 2021,
registered with PS Vadodbazar, District
Aurangabad Rural, Maharashtra, subject to
such terms and conditions, as may be
imposed by the Trial Court.
7.  Post  the  matter  immediately  after
partial court working days.”
6. Upon service of notice, the learned counsel
for the State has put in appearance.  She does not
dispute the fact that the appellant is an old
person and is visually blind.
7. Having regard to the aforesaid circumstances,
without expressing any opinion on the merits of
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.11290-11292/2026                                                           2

--- Page 136 ---
the appeal pending before the High Court, we deem
it appropriate to suspend the sentence awarded by
the  Trial  Court  pending  consideration  of  the
appeal of the appellant by the High Court. During
pendency of the appeal by the High Court, the
appellant shall continue to be on bail in terms
directed by the earlier order dated 09.06.2026.
8. With the aforesaid observations/directions,
these appeals are disposed of.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also
stand disposed of.

SLP (CRL) NO.8958/2026                         1/3

--- Page 150 ---
counsel appearing for the parties.
It  is  the  case  of  the
prosecution  that  the  appellant
along  with  other  co-accused
person(s)  has  committed  the

attributable to the appellant is
that  she  is  the  wife  of  the
deceased and along with accused
no.  1,  she  had  instigated  the
other  co-accused  person(s)to
commit  the  murder  after  proper
planning.

against the appellant are serious
in nature, we are inclined to set
aside the impugned order and grant
regular  bail  to  the  appellant,
considering the fact that she is a
lady and has a child to take care
of. 
SLP (CRL) NO.8958/2026                         2/3

--- Page 151 ---
Accordingly, the impugned order
passed by the High Court stands
set aside and the appellant is
granted bail subject to the terms
and conditions to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.

Trial Court may impose, is that
the appellant shall cooperate with
the pending proceedings before the
Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed,
accordingly.

any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

Miscellaneous Application No.  2041/2026 
@ Miscellaneous Application Diary No. 19982/2026 
in 
C.A. No. 13502/2025
SAROJ DEVI MEENA                                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION & ORS.             Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Through this application, the applicant-
Saroj  Devi  Meena  has  prayed  for  being
transposed  as  an  appellant  from  proforma
respondent No. 3 in disposed of Civil Appeal
No. 13502/2025.
3. The aforesaid Civil Appeal emanated from
a  judgment  of  the  Central  Administrative
Tribunal (“CAT”), Principal Bench, New Delhi
dated  15.05.2023  passed  in  five  connected
Original Applications (O.A.) filed by different
applicants.  Those  O.As  were  numbered
1394/2021, 1406/2021, 1368/2021, 3724/2022 and
1

--- Page 165 ---
173/2023.
4. The CAT, by its order dated 15.05.2023,
granted relief to all the original applicants.
5. Aggrieved by the order of the CAT, a Writ
Petition was filed before the High Court which
stood allowed by order dated 01.11.2023.
6. Against the order passed by the High
Court, Civil Appeal was filed before this Court
by  some  of  those  applicants  in  which  the
present applicant was impleaded as a proforma
respondent.  This  Court,  vide order  dated
10.11.2025, allowed the appeal(s) and set aside
the order passed by the High Court thereby
restoring the order passed by the CAT. In the
operative portion, it was mentioned that the
result of the appellants, who have participated
in the process of selection, be declared if
they are found meritorious.
7. The submission of the learned counsel for
the applicant is that once the order of the CAT
is  restored  after  setting  aside  the  order
passed by the High Court, all the applicants,
who were granted relief by the CAT, must have
the benefit of the order passed by this Court.
2

--- Page 166 ---
8. We  find  substance  in  the  aforesaid
submission. Consequently, IA No. 103068/2026
and miscellaneous application are disposed of
with a direction that the benefit of this
Court’s  order  dated  10.11.2025  would  be
available  to  all  those  applicants  whose
original application(s) were allowed by the
CAT.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION   (Civil) No.3274/2025  
RUPAM KUMARI PETITIONER
VERSUS
 BITTU KUMAR    RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Read the office report dated 13th July, 2026.
2. This  transfer petition,  under Section  25 of the  Code  of Civil
Procedure, 1908, has been presented by the petitioner-wife, Rupam
Kumari.
3. The  marriage  between  the  petitioner  and  the  respondent-
husband,  Bittu  Kumar,  was  solemnized  on  14th July,  2013.  Two
children were  born to the petitioner and the respondent from the
wedlock. However,  the  parties  have  since  drifted  apart owing  to
differences arising by and between them.
4. The respondent has instituted proceedings under Section 13-1(i)
(i-b) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 titled “ Bittu Kumar Vs. Rupam
Kumari” in the Family Court No.3, Nagpur, Maharashtra.
5. On the multiple grounds urged in the petition, the petitioner has
sought for transfer of the aforesaid proceedings to the Family Court,
1

--- Page 178 ---
Begusarai, Bihar.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We  are  satisfied,  having  regard  to  the  inconvenience  the
petitioner is likely to face if the proceedings were continued before
the  Family Court, Nagpur , Maharashtra,  which is at a distance of
around 1081 kms from her present place of residence,  that interest
of justice demands grant of the prayer for transfer. That apart, other
proceedings are also pending in the court at Begusarai, Bihar.
8. Hence, the transfer petition is allowed. Petition No. A/182/2025
titled “ Bittu Kumar Vs. Rupam Kumari ” pending before the  Family
Court No.3, Nagpur, Maharashtra  is transferred to the Family Court,
Begusarai, Bihar. Records of Petition No. A/182/2025 be transferred
to the transferee court immediately.
9. To avoid delay, the parties are directed to appear before the
transferee court on 12 th August, 2026. If any or both the parties fail
to appear on that day, the presiding officer of the transferee court
will fix another date of appearance. Thereafter, he shall be free to
proceed in accordance with law.
10. The presiding officer of the transferee court shall endeavour to
bring about a settlement between the parties through the process of
mediation. If the process is unsuccessful, nothing shall preclude the
presiding officer to take the proceedings to its logical conclusion on
its merits and in accordance with law.
11. If the respondent is unable to attend the proceedings in person
on any occasion, he will be at liberty to seek permission to attend
2

--- Page 179 ---
through the virtual mode and if such a permission is sought, the
presiding officer of the court may proceed to consider it reasonably.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.............................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
.............................J.
(SHEEL NAGU)
New Delhi;
July 14, 2026.
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

SANJAY SINGH                                       Appellant(s)

STATE OF HARYANA                                   Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3.This appeal arises from an order dated
08.05.2026  by  which  the  High  Court  has
rejected the regular bail prayer of the
appellant  in  connection  with  FIR  No.
843/2025, registered at P.S. Camp Palwal,
District Palwal. 
4. At the outset, learned counsel for the
appellant  submits  that  the  offences  for
which the appellant is being tried are all
1

--- Page 182 ---
triable  by  a  Court  of  Magistrate;
investigation is complete; and there is no
material on record to indicate that the
appellant would misuse the liberty of bail. 
5. The  learned  Counsel  for  the  State-
respondent has opposed the prayer for bail
however could not point out any previous
criminal antecedents of the appellant. 
6.  Having  regard  to  the  above,  without
expressing any opinion on the merits of the
case, we are of the view that the appellant
is entitled to be released on bail. 
7. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the
regular bail prayer of the appellant is set
aside.
8.The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court
may deem fit to impose in the facts and
2

--- Page 183 ---
circumstances of the case.
9.Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  1180/2015
UNION TERRITORY OF CHANDIGARH                    Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
PARMINDER JASWAL                                 Respondent(s)
WITH
Criminal Appeal  No(s). 1179/2015 
AND WITH
Criminal Appeal  No(s). 1178/2015 
O R D E R 
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. These  appeal(s)  impugn  judgment  and
order of the High Court of Punjab and Haryana
at  Chandigarh  dated  12.05.2011  passed  in
Criminal Misc. No. M-6437/2010 whereby the High
Court  has  quashed  an  FIR  No.  200  dated
25.08.2009,  registered  at  P.S.  Sector  11,
Chandigarh and all consequential proceedings on
the basis of a compromise between the parties.
1

--- Page 186 ---
3. A perusal of the record would reflect
that  FIR  was  lodged  by  alleging  that  the
complainant was sitting in the canteen of UIET
when members / office bearers of PUSU party
arrived, who were contesting election against a
member of SOPU party of which the complainant
was a member. Some arguments took place between
the members of the two parties regarding fixing
of posters. As a result, the complainant was
slapped and was also assaulted with a chair.
Though allegation is also that some gun shot
was fired at him, there is no medical report in
support  thereof.  Later,  the  charge  of  an
offence punishable under Section 307 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 (“IPC”) was altered to
one under Section 336 IPC.
4. As  parties  had  entered  into  a
compromise, taking note of the fact that the
accused and the complainant were young students
and the incident had taken place on account of
certain election issues, the High Court deemed
it appropriate to quash the proceedings as the
same  would  serve  no  useful  purpose  since
parties  were  not  willing  to  press  the
2

--- Page 187 ---
accusations.
5. We do not find any error in the view
taken by the High Court. The  appeal(s)  are,
accordingly, dismissed.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.    
  
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………..J
[VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi;
July 14, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                        O R D E R
SPECIAL LEAVE PETTION (CIVIL) NO(S). 4306 of 2026 
Pursuant to the order dated 19th May, 2026, the
respondent-father  was  allowed  to  exercise  his
visitation rights qua the minor children within the
premises of the Family Court, Bengaluru.  This Court
had also directed that the Family Court, Bengaluru
shall engage a counsellor to keep oversight during
the period when the father exercises the interim
custody/visitation rights qua the children by virtue
of the order passed by this Court. 
The following directions passed in the order
dated 19 th May, 2026 deal with the engagement of a
counsellor during the period of visitation and the
efforts required to be made by the counsellor to
facilitate the visitation sessions:-
“III.  An  experienced  counsellor  duly
2

--- Page 191 ---
identified by the Family Court, Bengaluru
shall be assigned to keep oversight during
the period when the father has the interim
custody of the children by virtue of this
order. 
IV. The counsellor shall observe and
determine comfort level of the children,
before the father is permitted to take the
children out of the premises of the Family
Court.
V. In case, the counsellor observes any
hesitations/apprehensions in either of the
children, he/she shall try to make the
children  comfortable  for  the  period  of
interim custody with the father.”
Shri  Siddharth  Bhatnagar,  learned  senior
counsel for the petitioner urged that the children
were  duly  presented  before  the  Family  Court,
Bengaluru in terms of this Court’s directions and
that the  father was  granted full  access to  the
children in presence of the counsellor.  However,
this submission is contested and controverted on some
particular  aspects  by  the  learned  counsel
representing the respondents.
In this background, we feel that a report of
the  counsellor  containing  the  observations  made
during the visitation sessions is necessary before
considering the custody arrangements of the children.
Accordingly,  we  direct  the  Family  Court,
Bengaluru to procure a report from the counsellor
concerned regarding the observations made during the
visitation sessions between the father and the two
3

--- Page 192 ---
minor children.  The report shall be forwarded to
this Court in a sealed envelope on or before the next
date of hearing i.e., 4th August, 2026.
A  copy  of  this  order  shall  be  forthwith
forwarded  to  the  Family  Court,  Bengaluru  for
compliance. 
List the matter again on 4th August, 2026.

O R D E R
I.A No.189431/2026 arising out of SLP (Civil) Diary Nos.18724 of
2026 and 19496 of 2026
1. Through this application the Government of Tamil Nadu has
made a request for permission to fill up vacancies in the
post of Chief Engineer (Public Works Department) by way
2

--- Page 203 ---
of temporary/ad hoc promotion under Section 47 (1) of the
Tamil Nadu Government Servants (Conditions of Service)
Act, 2016. 
2. The  learned  counsel  for  the  applicant  has  made  a
statement that such vacancies shall be filled up purely
on temporary/ ad hoc basis which shall be subject to the
outcome  of  the  pending  Special  Leave  Petitions.  He
further  states  that  such  appointment  shall  be  made
strictly in accordance with the seniority list, which was
redrawn, based on merits.
3. Having regard to the nature of the post concerned as also
that  leaving  the  said  post  vacant  would  cause
administrative difficulties, we are of the view that such
prayer can be accepted. Accordingly, we permit the State
Government of Tamil Nadu to fill up vacancies in the post
of Chief Engineer (Public Works Department) by way of
promotion on temporary/ ad hoc  basis in accordance with
the seniority list revised in terms of merit. Such
temporary arrangement shall, however, be subject to final
outcome of these pending Special Leave Petitions.
4. Let the bunch of these Special Leave Petitions be listed
for final hearing on 28.07.2026.
5. To facilitate the hearing, the learned counsel for the
parties shall submit brief written submissions providing
therein details of various orders passed in the context
of seniority lists, both in contempt as well as regular
3

--- Page 204 ---
proceedings. The submissions shall carry references of
annexure numbers and page numbers where those orders are
placed  in  the  paper  books  of  these  Special  Leave
Petitions.
I.A. No.191338/2026, I.A. Nos.195208 of 2026, I.A. No.189448 of
2026
Above-mentioned I.A.s are allowed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(C) No. 10160/2025
KUNAL MEHRA ..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
MANISHA MEHRA ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal arises out of the judgment dated 11.12.2024 passed
by the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi in First Appeal No.
247/2023, whereby the High Court upheld and confirmed the
dismissal of the appellant’s divorce case by the Court below.
3. During the pendency of this appeal, the parties were referred
to the Supreme Court Mediation Centre. Pursuant to the efforts
made by the learned Mediator, the parties settled their
disputes and executed Settlement Agreement dated 28.01.2026.
In terms thereof, they have now filed a joint application,
being I.A. No. 157681/2026, seeking appropriate directions
from this Court for dissolving their marriage. 
4. The marriage between the parties took place on 15.02.2013 and
they  have  two  sons,  born  on  23.01.2015  and  05.03.2020
respectively. They have, however, been living separately since
13.11.2020. There is, thus, no hope of any reconciliation
1

--- Page 206 ---
between the couple at this stage. 
5. Given the facts and the settlement of disputes by and between
the parties, we find no reason to prolong their marital tie or
keep  the  litigation  initiated  by  them  pending.   We,
accordingly, exercise our jurisdiction under Article 142 of
the Constitution of India and dissolve the marriage performed
by and between them on 15.02.2013. The pending cases instituted
by them against each other shall also be brought to a closure.
6. We may note that Complaint Case No. 1022/2021 was filed by the
respondent-wife, titled “ Manisha Mehra vs. Kunal Mehra and
Ors”,  now Miscellaneous Criminal Application No. 7933/2025
pending on the file of the learned Chief Judicial Magistrate,
Hazaribagh, Jharkhand.  That apart, the respondent-wife also
filed Criminal Revision Case No. 1107 of 2023, which is
pending before the High Court of Jharkhand at Ranchi. This
criminal revision case arose out of Maintenance Case No.
29/2023, filed by her before the learned Principal Judge,
Family Court, Hazaribagh, Jharkhand.
7. Both the aforestated cases shall stand closed in the light of
this order and the Settlement Agreement dated 28.01.2026
between the parties. 
8. In terms of the Settlement Agreement, the appellant-husband
remitted the sum of 35,00,000/- (Rupees thirty five lakhs₹
only) directly into the bank account of the respondent-wife in
Punjab National Bank, details of which have been furnished in
paragraph 3 of the Affidavit in Compliance dated 10.07.2026
2

--- Page 207 ---
filed by the appellant.  He also created two fixed deposits on
06.07.2026 in the names of his two sons, namely, Viraj Mehra
and Yuvraj Mehra, each for a sum of 25,00,000/- (Rupees₹
twenty five lakhs only), and delivered the same to the
respondent-wife. This amount is to be spent for the welfare
and education of the sons. We may, however, make it clear that
payment of these amounts towards the welfare and education of
the sons would not have the effect of extinguishing or
adversely affecting their legal rights, if any, be it in
relation to property or and against their father and his
family in any other respect. 
9. We also make it clear that, if any of the terms and conditions
of the Settlement Agreement are not honoured by either of the
parties, it would be open to the party aggrieved to seek
specific performance of the Settlement Agreement before the
appropriate forum in accordance with law. 
10. I.A. 157681/2026 and the appeal are disposed of in the
aforestated terms.
11. The Registry shall prepare a decree for dissolution of the
marriage by mutual consent in terms of this order.
12.Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
.....................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
.....................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.         OF 2026
(ARISING OUT OF SLP (CIVIL) NO. 13824 OF 2026)
T. PONPRAKASH APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE CHIEF SECRETARY TO 
GOVERNMENT & ANR.
RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant joined the Tamil Nadu State Judicial Service on
01.07.1991 as Civil Judge (Junior Division). It seems that after
serving for more than 25 years and while he was working as a Judge
at  Family  Court,  Dindigul,  the  appellant  was  placed  under
suspension  on  29.01.2016,  followed  by  a  chargesheet  dated
12.09.2016.  There were 12 charges, inter alia, alleging that the
appellant,  while  functioning  as  a  Principal  District  Judge,
Ramanathapuram, demanded a silver plate from the Sub Court. He is
also  alleged  to  have  demanded  a  gold  ring  to  conduct  the
inspection. Eventually, during the inquiry, it was held that the
appellant demanded various items, such as a Raymond Safari set, dry
fruits, and other articles, worth Rs. 20,000/-. These incidents
took  place  during  the  years  2012-2013.  In  the  next  annual
inspection year, 2014-2015, the appellant was also stated to have
received seven silver plates, an air conditioner, a water heater,
Wi-Fi,  some  sarees,  a  gold  ring,  etc.  The  allegations  were
eventually proved in part  vide inquiry report dated 28.07.2017.
1

--- Page 210 ---
Thereafter,  the  Administrative  Committee  accepted  the  inquiry
report on 24.11.2019 and, having found the appellant guilty of
committing serious misconduct, a major penalty of dismissal from
service was imposed. Those recommendations were approved by the
Full Court of the High Court on 07.12.2019, followed by the
Government Order dated 08.02.2020, dismissing the appellant from
service. The appellant’s challenge to that order of dismissal
having been turned down by the High Court, he has approached this
Court.
3. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant as well as
learned counsel for the High Court.
4. Though, in light of the inquiry report dated 28.07.2017, in
terms whereof, some of the allegations causing serious aspersion on
the integrity and impropriety of the appellant having been proved,
there can be no quarrel that the resolution of the High Court to
the extent of imposing a major penalty was fully justified. The
only factor that seems to be missing is whether the High Court
considered the desirability of imposing a lesser major penalty,
like compulsory retirement from service, etc.
5. Taking into consideration the total service of around 25
years, it seems to us that the issue re: quantum of appropriate
major penalty deserves reconsideration. We, however, do not want to
impose any penalty in substitution for the order of dismissal
passed by the High Court. Consequently, the instant appeal is
allowed in part to the limited extent that the Full Court of the
High Court is requested to reconsider the quantum of punishment
imposed on the appellant. It is, however, reiterated that it shall
2

--- Page 211 ---
be the discretion of the High Court to impose a suitable and
appropriate punishment befitting the nature of the charges proved
against the appellant. In this regard, we have not expressed any
opinion on the merits of the case.  The High Court is requested to
take an appropriate decision within a period of three months.
..........................CJI.
       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
       (JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
..........................J.
       (V. MOHANA) 
          
 
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026.
3

--- Page 212 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION         OF 2026
[Diary No(s).50534/2025]
IN
CIVIL APPEAL NO.6646/2024
DELHI DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
TANVIR BEGUM & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. This application has been moved by the Delhi Development
Authority seeking an extension of a further six months for
completion  of  the  acquisition  process  in  terms  of  the
judgment dated 17.05.2024. Though we are not satisfied with
the explanation rendered by the Authorities, it appears to
be a case of gross negligence on the part of the officers to
whom  the  responsibility  of  the  acquisition  has  been
entrusted. However, in the interest of the pending projects
and  taking  into  consideration  the  totality  of  the
circumstances,  three  months  further  time  from  today  is
granted to complete the acquisition process, failing which
there shall be exemplary costs to be personally recoverable
from the officers.
3. As such, the acquisition process shall be completed
within  the  extended  time  period,  and  the  amount  of
compensation, as per the award, shall be deposited with the
1

--- Page 214 ---
Prescribed Authority within four weeks.
4. The Miscellaneous Application is, accordingly, disposed
of.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION………………. Diary No(s). 50534/2025

1. We have heard learned counsel/Senior Counsel for the parties.
2. We have no reason to doubt that Respondent Nos. 6-10 in W.P.
(C) No.460/2025, namely, Samay Raina, Vipul Goyal, Balraj Paramjeet
Singh Ghai, Sonali Thakkar aka Sonali Aditya Desai, and Nishant
Jagdish Tanwar have acted in brazen violation of the undertakings
and statements made before this Court. The misconduct is sought to
3

--- Page 232 ---
be further compounded by the fact that no recent affidavit of
compliance has been placed on record, while attempting to portray
that such affidavit has already been filed. Instead, the learned
counsel sought to handover copies of the same only when this matter
was taken up for hearing.
3. Subject to costs of Rs.3 lakhs each, to be deposited by the
said Respondents with the Registry of this Court within two weeks,
a period of 15 days is granted to file the compliance affidavit(s).
It is made clear that if the costs are not deposited within the
stipulated  time,  coercive  action  shall  be  taken  against  the
person(s) in default.
4. Post the matters on 14.08.2026.
  
(ARJUN BISHT)                              (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.12996/2025
VIMLA DEVI                              Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
KARNATAKA BANK LIMITED                 Respondent(s)
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NO.171/2026
O R D E R
We had passed an order on 30.06.2026, after
hearing  both  the  sides,  clearly  indicating  our
disinclination to allow both the appeals and the
interim  applications  filed.  It  is  only  for  the
purpose of facilitating the learned counsel for the
appellant(s) to get certain instructions, that time
was granted. 
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the appellants and the learned counsel appearing for
the respondent.

--- Page 250 ---
2
Upon  hearing  them,  we  are  in  respectful
agreement with the impugned order passed by the
National Company Law Appellate Tribunal and the order
passed by the National Company Law Tribunal, that the
present proceedings are nothing but an abuse of the
process of law. These proceedings only show the
anxiety of the appellants to hold onto the property
by taking recourse to law.
Considering the above, we do not find any reason
to interfere with the impugned orders.
The appeals stand dismissed, accordingly.
Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
………………………………………....J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
………………………………………....J.
[SHEEL NAGU]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 03, 2026.

Rest of the matters.

submitted  that  letter  dated  01.10.2024  seeking  certain

Enterprises Pvt. Ltd. submitted that on receipt of the said

STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                             RESPONDENT(S)
(IA No. 137722/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED  
JUDGMENT
IA No. 137723/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 13-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s): Mr. Chritarth Palli , AOR
  
For Respondent(s): Mr. Vikas Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Nagarkatti Kartik Uday, AOR
                   Mr. Umang K. Khosla, Adv.
                   Ms. Shreyasi Khosla, Adv. 
  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioners.
2. Applications seeking exemption from filing the  
certified copy of the impugned judgment and official  
translation are allowed.
3. The  petitioners  are  the  in-laws  of  the  
complainant-respondent No.2. We find that the lodging  
of the FIR has the genesis in the matrimonial dispute  
between son of the petitioners and the complainant.
1

--- Page 258 ---
4. In the present proceedings, today Mr. Nagarkatti  
Kartik Uday has appeared on behalf of the complainant-
respondent No.2 on caveat.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that  
an  endeavour  may  be  made  for  resolution  of  the  
matrimonial dispute between the parties.
6. On a query by the Court, learned counsel for the  
complainant-respondent No.2 submits that a divorce case 
has been lodged by the son of the petitioners in  
Kolkata in respect of which the complainant has filed a 
transfer petition before this Court bearing  T.P.(C) 
No.361/2026 titled as “Ritika Gohri versus Siddharth  
Sharma”.  In  the  circumstances,  we  issue  notice  
returnable on 06.08.2026.
7. Tag this matter along with T.P.(C) No.361/2026.
8. In the meantime, there shall be stay of further  
proceedings in FIR No.22 dated 16.06.2025 registered at 
Women Police Station, Patiala, Punjab.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                 (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA NO.113896 OF 2026:
This application has been filed by the petitioner in
these Special Leave Petitions seeking in substance the
renewal of passport of the minor daughter-Miraya (United
States Passport) and consequentially to renew/extend her
OCI Card etc.
Learned senior counsel for the applicant/petitioner
in  these  Special  Leave  Petitions  submitted  that  the
applicant is not pressing the first portion of the prayer
in the application as the petitioner is interested in the
renewal  of  Miraya’s  United  States’  passport  and
consequentially extension of her OCI Card.
We  have  heard  learned  senior  counsel  for  the
applicant/petitioner  and  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent.
It is stated at the bar that 17.07.2026 is the date
indicated  by  the  United  States’  Embassy  for  the
petitioner-wife and her daughter to be present for the
2

--- Page 263 ---
interview for the purpose of seeking renewal of the United
States passport and therefore, permission may be granted
to her to take all necessary steps for seeking extension
of the passport of the minor daughter.
Per  contra,  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent-
husband submitted that his party has also sought date for
the interview on 24.07.2026, which is on the basis of the
order of the Canadian court for the very same purpose and
therefore, the applicant/petitioner and the minor daughter
may appear before the Embassy on 24.07.2026.
We have considered the submissions made at the bar.
Since 17.07.2026 is the initial date indicated by the
Embassy for the petitioner-wife and the minor daughter to
be present, we find that on the said date they must be
present before the Embassy lest an adverse impression is
created with regard to the process of the renewal of the
passport.  Further,  all  steps  may  be  taken  by  the
petitioner-wife for the renewal of the passport and for
extension of the OCI Card.
We  also  observe  that  the  steps  taken  by  the
petitioner-wife in this regard in the absence of the
respondent-husband would have no bearing on the custody
issue pending before this Court. This order has been made
purely in the best interest of the minor child-Miraya so
that  her  United  States’  Passport  is  renewed  and
consequentially the OCI Card is also extended.
Application is disposed of in the aforesaid terms.
3

--- Page 264 ---
Since  the  respondent-husband  is  in  India  from
20.07.2026  onwards  for  a  period  of  three  weeks,  the
parties to work out amicably visitation of the father vis-
a-vis the minor daughter.
List on 17.07.2026.
(B. LAKSHMI MANIKYA VALLI)                      (DIVYA BABBAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).
7937/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 31-01-2026
in  MCRC  No.  10028/2025  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
