HARPRASAD JANGDE                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                          Respondent(s)
IA No. 132965/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing 
 today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Abhinav Shrivastava, AOR
Mr. Amit Singh Chauhan, Adv.
Ms. Unnati Vaibhav, Adv.
Ms. Barkha Agrawal, Adv.
Ms. Dibya Kumari Rai, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) Mr. Vinayak Sharma, Standing counsel
      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Ms. Barkha Agrawal,  learned counsel for the
petitioner and Mr. Vinayak Sharma, learned counsel for
the State.
2. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the
correctness of the order  dated 31-01-2026 in MCRC

--- Page 269 ---
2
No.10028/2025 passed by the High Court of Chhattisgarh
at Bilaspur. By the said order, the petitioner has been
denied the benefit of bail in connection with Crime
No.18/2025 registered at Police Station -Cyber Police
Thana Range, District-Bilaspur, Chhattisgarh for the
offences  punishable  under  Sections  61(2),  317(5),
318(4), 323 & 111(4) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita,
2023.
3. We have perused the counter affidavit, handed over
by the learned counsel for the State. The same is taken
on record.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
petitioner has already undergone about one year as an
under trial. Learned counsel also drawing attention to
the chargesheet and submits that there is no money
which has come into the account of the petitioner and
that the petitioner has been brought into the case on
account of the confession of a co-accused Dharmendra
Prasad  Suryavanshi.  There  been  certain  telephonic
conversation between him and the petitioner, which has
been relied upon in the chargesheet.
5. Learned counsel for the State vehemently opposes the
bail prayer of the petitioner. 
6. Considering the overall facts and circumstances and
the period already undergone by the petitioner and the
likelihood of the trial taking some time, we are
inclined to enlarge the petitioner on bail pending

--- Page 270 ---
3
trial.
7. Accordingly,  we  direct  that  pending  trial  the
petitioner shall  be enlarged on bail su bject to the
satisfaction of the concerned Trial Court in connection
with the aforesaid Crime case and for the offences
mentioned hereinabove.
8. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                             (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)
*Counter affidavit is attached with RoP

ITEM NO.6           COURT NO.14               SECTION II-A
            U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                  RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).
9470/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  06-04-
2026 in BA No. 1685/2025 passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Bombay]
JESABHAI MOTABHAI MALI                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MAHARASHTRA                        Respondent(s)
IA No. 155919/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF I/JUDGMENT
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing 
 today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Shantanu Phanse, Adv.
                   Ms. Preet Phanse, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddhant Sharma, AOR                 
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Varad Kilor, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Dharmadhikari, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, AOR
                   Mr. Shrirang B. Varma, Adv.
                   
     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Shantanu Phanse, learned counsel for the
petitioner and Mr. Varad Kilor, learned counsel for the
State.
2. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the
correctness of the order  dated 06-04-2026 in BA No.

--- Page 272 ---
2
1685/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay. By the said order, the petitioner has been
denied the benefit of bail in connection with Crime
No.12/2024 registered with ANC, Ghatkopar Unit, Mumbai,
Maharashtra, for the offences punishable under Sections
8(C), 22(C) & 29 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic
Substances Act, 1985 (for short, ‘NDPS’).
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
petitioner has already completed about two years and
four months as an under trial. Learned counsel submits
that taking the prosecution’s case at its highest is
that the petitioner was a financial conduit and ran an
international  drug  syndicate ,  who  accelerated  the
movement of proceeds of crime through hawala channel.
Learned counsel for the petitioner denies his role.
Learned counsel submits that there is no allegation
that the petitioner was directly concerned with the
contraband. Learned counsel submits that the total
number of listed witnesses are 127 and the trial is
likely to take a very long time to conclude.
4. Learned counsel for the State vehemently opposes the
grant  of  bail  to  the  petitioner.  Learned  counsel
submits that petitioner has provided financial support
and as such he ought not to be enlarged on bail.
5. We have considered the facts and circumstances of
the case and also kept in mind the ingredients of
Section 37 of NDPS. Considering the role attributed to

--- Page 273 ---
3
the petitioner; the period of custody that is already
undergone and the number of witnesses that are to be
examined as also the likelihood of trial would take a
very  long  time;  coupled  with  the  fact  that  even
according to the prosecution, there was no direct
involvement with the contraband, we are inclined to
enlarge the petitioner on bail.  
6. In view of the above, we direct that the petitioner
shall be enlarged on bail su bject to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court in connection with the
aforesaid Crime case and for the offences mentioned
hereinabove.
7. At  this  stage,  learned  counsel  for  the  State
mentioned that prosecution is in Maharashtra and the
petitioner is in Gujarat, the likelihood the petitioner
will abscond. 
8. The  Trial  Court  will  make  sure  that  stringent
conditions are imposed including the condition that the
petitioner  will  surrender  his  passport,  if  he  is
possession of the one.
9. With  the  above  observations,  the  Special  Leave
Petition is disposed of.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                             (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)

Date : 14-07-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) : Appellant-in-person, AOR
                    Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present miscellaneous application(s) has been
filed against the order dated 12.02.2025, passed by the
Registrar,  declining  to  receive  the  miscellaneous
1

--- Page 275 ---
application(s), filed for re-hearing of the matter.
2. The facts of the case reveal that the petitioner has
earlier filed a Writ Petition i.e. Writ Petition (Civil)
No. 274 of 2024 and the same was dismissed by this Court by
an order dated 13.05.2024. The order dated 13.05.2024 reads
as under :
“1. Application seeking permission to appear
and argue-in-person is granted.
2. Heard the petitioner appearing as party-
in-person and perused the record.
3. We are not inclined to entertain the Writ
Petition  filed by  the petitioner-in-person
under  Article  32  of  the  Constitution  of
India.
4. It will be open for the petitioner to file
appropriate  proceedings  before  the
appropriate forum as may be permitted under
the law.
5. Subject to the above observations, the
Writ Petition is dismissed.”
3. The aforesaid order makes it very clear that this
Court has declined to entertain the writ petition filed by
the  petitioner-in-person  under  Article  32  of  the
Constitution of India and liberty was granted to the
petitioner  to  file  appropriate  proceedings  before  the
appropriate forum as may be permitted under the law.
4. The  petitioner  has  now  filed  the  present
miscellaneous application(s) and again wants this Court to
decide the issue.
5. In the considered opinion of this Court, once a
2

--- Page 276 ---
liberty  has  been  granted  to  the  petitioner  to  file
appropriate proceedings before the appropriate forum as may
be permitted under the law, the Registrar (Judicial) has
rightly  declined  to  receive  the  miscellaneous
application(s)  filed  in  the  matter.  Accordingly,  the
miscellaneous application(s) are dismissed. The petition
preferred under Article 32 of the Constitution of India is
also dismissed with the aforesaid liberty. 
6. Again liberty is granted to the petitioner to file
appropriate proceedings before the appropriate forum as may
be permitted under the law.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                    (KOMAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. I.A. No.169333/2026, i.e., application for permission
to file Miscellaneous Application is allowed.
2. Delay condoned.
3. This Miscellaneous Application is at the instance of
the High Court at Calcutta through the Registrar General,
seeking clarification of our order dated 09.02.2026.
4. Our order dated 09.02.2026 reads thus:-
1

--- Page 284 ---
M.A. D. No.32058/2026
“1. This petition arises from an interim order
passed by a Division Bench of the High Court of
Calcutta dated 19.01.2026 by which the High Court
while declining to grant any relief to the party
who  is  alleged  to  have  put  up  unauthorized
construction proceeded to observe in paras 21, 22,
23 & 24 respectively as under:-
“21. The  BMC  shall  explain  in  their
affidavit as to why no steps have been
taken to stop the construction work in the
said building since 2022. The officials
responsible to conduct the affairs of the
BMC from Mayor to Assistant Engineer shall
be named in the affidavit of the BMC.
22. It is also submitted by the counsel
for  the  parties  that  several  such
buildings  without  sanction  plan  are
existing within the territorial limits of
the BMC.
23. In their affidavit the BMC shall also
explain what steps it is taking against
such unauthorized constructions.
24. A cascading effect is likely to occur
as  a  consequence  of  the  aforesaid
unauthorized construction. It is not known
as to how the municipal amenities would be
given to these unauthorized construction
and where the funds therefor would come
from. No taxes can be collected from the
residents of an unauthorized construction.
There are several other questions that
emerge that shall be addressed on the
adjourned date.”
2. We heard Dr. S. Muralidhar, the learned senior
counsel appearing for the petitioner corporation.
3. The learned senior counsel brought to our
notice that the Court of the Hon’ble Chief Justice
of the High Court is in  seisin of all matters
relating to unauthorized construction within the
limits of Bidhannagar Municipal Corporation.
4. While declining to interfere with the interim
order,  we  may  only  say  that  let  all  matters
relating to unauthorized construction be heard by
2

--- Page 285 ---
M.A. D. No.32058/2026
the Court of the Hon’ble Chief Justice of the
Calcutta High Court.
5. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition
stand disposed of.
6. Pending application(s), if any, also stand
disposed of.”
5. The learned counsel appearing for the High Court made
a fervent appeal that para 4 is posing administrative
difficulties.
6. In para 4 of our order, we said that let all matters
relating to unauthorized construction be heard by the
Court of the Hon’ble Chief Justice of the High Court.
However,  according  to  the  learned  counsel,  there  are
hundreds of matters which are being heard by different
Courts in the High Court, more particularly, in accordance
with the roster.
7. We modify para 4 of our order referred to above,
saying that let the matters be heard in accordance with
the roster.
8. With  the  aforesaid,  the  Miscellaneous  Application
stands disposed of.
9. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  also  stand
disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

1. We have heard learned senior counsel for the petitioner
and learned counsel for the respondent-Institution,
Roorkee. We have also perused the extract of the
Minutes of the 10th Meeting of the Senate in the matter
of transfer of a student from an Indian Institute of
1

--- Page 287 ---
Technology to any other Indian Institute of Technology.
2. In the instant case, it is noted that the petitioner
cannot avail of his transfer to an Indian Institute
Technology in Delhi on medical grounds owing to the
difference  in  the  curriculum  B.  Arch.  Course.
Therefore, learned senior counsel submitted that the
petitioner may be transferred to the Indian Institute
of Technology, Roorkee. However, for that purpose, it
is necessary that there should be a Medical Board
constituted to examine the petitioner at the place from
where the migration is not before recommending the
migration.
3. Learned counsel for the Indian Institute of Technology,
Kharagpur submitted that they have no objection for the
petitioner being transferred from Indian Institute of
Technology, Kharagpur to any other institution.
4. In the circumstances, we direct the petitioner to
appear  before  the  All  India  Institute  of  Medical
Sciences, New Delhi (AIIMS) on 20.07.2026 so that the
petitioner is evaluated medically and the report is
submitted to this Court as we think it is not feasible
in the circumstances to direct the petitioner to travel
Indian  Institute  of  Technology,  Kharagpur for  the
aforesaid purpose.
5. The Director of AIIMS shall constitute a Medical Board
which shall submit its report to this Court.
2

--- Page 288 ---
6. Since the last date for admission is stated to be on
17.07.2026, we direct IIT, Roorkee to keep one seat
vacant subject to the result of this Writ Petition.

this Court to the Director, AIIMS for compliance.

Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee.

MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION Diary No. 27357/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 12-02-2026
in C.A. No. No. 174/2013 passed by the Supreme Court of India]
SANT LAL                                           Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ANR.                              Respondent(s)
IA No. 139581/2026 - EXTENSION OF TIME
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. N Venkataraman, A.S.G.
                    Mr. Gurmeet Singh Makker, AOR
                    Mrs. Nisha Bagchi, Sr. Adv.
                    Mr. Arijit Prasad, Sr. Adv.
                    Mr. Amitesh Chandra, Adv.
                    Mr. Navanjay Mahapatra, Adv.
                    Mrs. Alka Agarwal, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard Mr. Arijit Prasad, learned senior counsel
appearing for the applicant-Union of India.
2. Having regard to the order which we propose to pass, issuance
of notice is considered not necessary.
3. This  Miscellaneous  Application  has  been  filed  seeking
extension of time to comply with the Order dated 12.02.2026, passed
1

--- Page 293 ---
by this Court in Civil Appeal No.174/2013 ( Sant Lal vs. Union of
India & Anr ). Operative portion of the judgment and order dated
12.02.2026 reads as follows:
“14. Accordingly, we grant the relief in the
following terms:
a. The benefit as extended to respondent no.2
has not been disturbed and he will continue
to get the same benefit as allowed to him.
b.  The respondents are directed to absorb
the appellant as Court Master with effect
from the date of his relieving while he was
serving  on  deputation  in  the  DRT.  After
relieving, since the appellant continued in
the  CAT  till  attaining  the  age  of
superannuation, therefore, on his absorption
in the DRT with effect from the date of his
relieving, he may not be entitled to get
difference of salary. It is directed that
treating  him  absorbed  as  Court  Master  on
higher scale at par to respondent no.2, his
consequential notional benefit be calculated.
According to the said calculation, he would
be entitled to get post retiral pensionary
benefits.  The  pension  be  also  fixed
accordingly; and
c. On fixation of post retiral pensionary
benefits, difference of pension be also paid
to him. The aforesaid exercise be completed
within a period of two months.”
4. Though this application for extension of time was filed after
expiry of the period of two months as directed by this Court,
nonetheless, we are of the view that a further period of three
months may be granted to the applicant to implement the judgment
and order of this Court dated 12.02.2026.
5. Ordered accordingly.
6. Consequently time is extended till 31.10.2026 for compliance
of the judgment and order of this Court dated 12.02.2026 passed in
2

--- Page 294 ---
Civil Appeal No. 174/2013.
7. Miscellaneous Application is disposed of.
8. Pending applicant(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
(IRAMNAZ)                                       (CHETNA BALOONI)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 295 ---

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  1082/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 29-11-2024 in
CRLM No. 49347/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna]
THE UNION OF INDIA                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SUBHASH PRASAD YADAV                               Respondent(s)
WITH
SLP(Crl) No. 1079/2026 (II-A)
SLP(Crl) No. 1048/2026 (II-A)
Date : 14-07-2026 These petitions were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Tushar Mehta, Solicitor General
                   Mr. K.M. Nataraj, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Annam Venkatesh, Adv.
                   Mr. Arkaj Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Vidushi Pandey, Adv.
                   Ms. Mili Joy Baxi, Adv.
                   Ms. Astha Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Digvijay Dam, Adv.
                   Mr. Arvind Kumar Sharma, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Anshuman Sinha, Adv.
                   Mr. Vijay Kumar Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Vinay Prakash, Adv.
                   Mr. Udayan Sinha, Adv.
                   Mr. Prakhar Prakash, Adv.
                   Ms. Pragya Sharma, AOR 
Mr. Pankaj Bhagat, AOR                         
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. These three Special Leave Petitions are directed against the
1

--- Page 296 ---
order dated 29.11.2024 passed by the learned Single Judge of the
Patna High Court, granting regular bail to the private respondents
in the cases emerging from ECIR/PTZO/14/2023 registered by the
Directorate  of  Enforcement  (ED),  Patna  Zone  Office,  against
Broadson  Commodities  Private  Limited  (BSCPL)  and  its
Directors/former Directors under Sections 3 and 4 of Prevention of
Money Laundering Act, 2002. The allegations pertain to generating
proceeds of crime by means of illicit mining and selling of sand.
The ED has already filed Prosecution Complaints and the trials are
pending before the Special Court (PMLA), Patna.
2. The record reveals that Subhash Prasad Yadav was arrested on
09.03.2024, while Surendra Kumar Jindal and Baban Singh were
arrested on 03.11.2023.
3. It is true that the High Court has released the respondents on
regular bail after hardly one year of incarceration. It appears
that there are 17 accused in the predicate offence, out of whom the
case under the PMLA has been lodged against the private respondents
and some other persons. However, the principles with respect to
interference with an order granting bail are well established, and
unless the order is found to be perverse or contrary to law or it
becomes an impediment in the ongoing investigation or trial, this
Court would be extremely reluctant to interfere with the same
merely on the ground that the High Court ought not to have
exercised its discretion at an early stage.
4. In this regard, learned counsel for the private respondents
undertake that the said respondents, as well as their defense
counsel, will extend full cooperation to the ongoing investigation
2

--- Page 297 ---
as well as the trial, as the case may be, and there shall be no
adjournment sought by them without any valid ground. That being so,
we are not inclined to interfere with the impugned order of the
High Court at this stage. Suffice it to say that so long as the
private respondents continue to cooperate for the expeditious
conclusion of the pending proceedings, it will be difficult to
infer that there is misuse of concession of bail. However, if it is
found, in the future, that the respondents themselves or through
local counsel are misusing concession, the petitioner(s) shall be
at  liberty  to  approach  the  High  Court  or  this  Court  for
cancellation of bail granted to the private respondents.
5. With this liberty, the Special Leave Petitions stand disposed
of.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand closed.
(NITIN TALREJA)                             (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
3

--- Page 298 ---

the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Sections 7 and 12 of the
Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988. 
2. The above-stated FIR has been registered on the basis of
a  communication  received  under  Section  66(2)  of  the
Prevention  of  Money  Laundering  Act,  2002,  from  the
Enforcement Directorate, alleging a larger conspiracy in
allotment of DMF-related contracts.
3. The allegations against the petitioner are that he acted
as a key intermediary-cum-commission agent in agricultural-
related procurement under the DMF framework since 2019. The
vendors, who wanted work orders, had to pay him a 30 to 35%
commission, of which he kept 20 to 25% for himself, and the
remainder  was  forwarded  upward  to  the  syndicate.
Approximately Rs.5 crore is alleged to have been received by
the  petitioner  as  commission,  routed  through  the  bank
accounts of his family members and associated firms.
4. The petitioner was arrested on 18.02.2026 and has been
in custody since then. During the course of the hearing,
learned counsel for the parties have fairly admitted that one
of the main accused (Anil Tuteja) has already been released
by this Court on regular bail in the subject case vide order
dated 18.05.2026 passed in SLP(Crl.) No.7878/2026. Some other
co-accused have already been released. 
5. The conclusion of the trial will take some time. No
purpose will be served by keeping the petitioner in judicial
custody.
2

--- Page 303 ---
6. Consequently, without expressing any opinion on merits,
the prayer for bail is allowed, and the petitioner is ordered
to be released on bail subject to his furnishing bail bonds
to the satisfaction of the Trial Court and also subject to
such conditions which have already been imposed on the
petitioner’s co-accused.
7. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.
8. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed
of.
(ARJUN BISHT)                         (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(Crl.) No. 16464/2025)
MOTI RAM ..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
MAHENDRA KUMAR SHARMA (SARASWAT) & 
ORS. 
..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  report  dated  09.06.2026  received  from  the
Registrar(Judl.), Rajasthan High Court, Jodhpur, confirms that
the appellant, Moti Ram, who was arrayed as respondent No. 2
in S.B. Criminal Writ Petition No. 409/2024, was not even put
on notice prior to the disposal of the said case by way of the
impugned order dated 23.08.2024.
3. In that view of the matter, as the High Court ought not to
have decided the matter against the appellant, Moti Ram,
without even affording him an opportunity of hearing, we are
constrained to set aside the impugned order dated 23.08.2024
on that short ground. 
4. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed,  setting  aside  the
impugned order. S.B. Criminal Writ Petition No. 409/2024 shall
stand restored to the file of the High Court. It shall be
disposed of on merits after putting all the contesting parties
1

--- Page 311 ---
on notice and affording them an opportunity of hearing.  
5. To cut short valuable time, we request the Registrar of the
High Court to ensure that the matter is listed before the
appropriate Bench on 07.09.2026 for further steps to be taken
therein.  The  parties  are  directed  to  appear  before  the
designated Bench of the High Court on the said date.
6. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
.....................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)

Delhi, she will be entitled to reimbursement from the respondent-
husband towards her boarding, lodging and transportation cost,
which we quantify at Rs.15,000/- (Rupees Fifteen Thousand) per
visit and the same shall be transferred into the bank account of
the petitioner-wife by the respondent-husband at least four days
prior to the date fixed for her physical appearance.

period of two months thereafter.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 06-05-2026

at Chandigarh]
GAGANDEEP SINGH                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                             Respondent(s)
(IA No. 153529/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT
IA No. 153530/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Naresh Chhokar, Adv.
                   Mr. Ram Bhati, Adv.
                   Mr. Kailas Bajirao Autade, AoR
                   Mr. Shobhit Dwivedi, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Karan Sharma, AoR
                   Mr. Rajat Jain, Adv.                   
                   
                   Mr. Siddharth Aggarwal, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Gurfateh Singh Khosa, Adv.
                   Mr. Anmol Kheta, Adv.
                   Mr. Vishwajeet Bhati, Adv.
                   Ms. Tanya Srivastava, AoR                       
          
                             O R D E R
Heard Mr. Naresh Chhokar, learned counsel for the petitioner,
Mr. Karan Sharma, learned Advocate-on-Record for the respondent

--- Page 317 ---
2
no.1-State of Punjab and Mr. Siddharth Aggarwal, learned senior
counsel for the respondent no.2.
2. Earlier on 21.05.2026, while issuing notice, we had directed
that if the respondent no.2 has not yet left India, he shall not do
so without specific orders from this Court. This was in view of the
fact that by the impugned order passed by the High Court, the
respondent no.2 had been granted permission to travel abroad upon
certain conditions fixed by the High Court.
3. Having heard learned senior counsel/counsel for the parties,
we find that sufficient safeguards have been ensured by the High
Court for appearance of respondent no.2 as and when he may be
required by the Court concerned.
4. Thus, taking into account the fact that the respondent no.2 is
a citizen of United States of America and his family is residing
there, the purpose of allowing him to be on bail would be of no
meaning if he is not allowed to be in the company of his family and
to move on with his profession in the United States of America, at
least  till  the  time  his  presence  is  not  required  in  the
proceedings.
5. Learned senior counsel for the respondent no.2 assures that as
and when the Court requires the physical presence of respondent
no.2, he shall comply with the said directions.
6. Recording that, in addition to the conditions already set by
the High Court in the impugned order, we do not find any reason to
interfere in the impugned order.
7. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
8. At this stage, learned senior counsel for the respondent no.2

--- Page 318 ---
3
submitted that the period for which, the permission to travel
abroad was granted having substantially expired, he may suitably
compensated.
9. Having regard to the facts and circumstances of the case, we
find substance in the stand taken by learned senior counsel for the
respondent no.2.
10. Accordingly, the period for which, the High Court had allowed
the respondent no.2 to go back to the United States of America
stands extended till 31st December, 2026.
11. Needless to say that in view of the present order, all
consequential action taken by the State Authorities in terms of the
interim order passed by this Court on 21.05.2026 shall be withdrawn
and the said order also stands vacated.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

THE STATE OF W.B.                                  Respondent(s)

IA No. 155937/2026 - CLARIFICATION/DIRECTION
 
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Appellant(s) : Mr. Gaurav Agarwal, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Aarif Ali, Adv.

Mr. swapnil Pattanayak, Adv. 
Ms. Kratika Kushwaha, Adv. 
Mr. Kunal Mimani, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA No.155937/2026:
1. This Court by order dated 06.04.2026 enlarged the appellant on
bail and permitted the trial court to impose such terms and
conditions for his release. Pursuant to the same, the trial court
by order dated 16.04.2026 has ordered as under:
“That the convict / appellant Mujammil Sk. @ Akram @
Akka may find bail of Rs. 50,000/- (rupees fifty
thousand only) with two surety each of Rs. 25,000/-
1

--- Page 320 ---
(rupees twenty-five thousands only) each, one must
be local having sufficient landed property of the
same valuation subject to satisfaction of learned
C.J.M. South 24-Parganas at Alipore with condition
that he shall not leave the jurisdiction of Tiljala
P.S and to provide local address to the Officer-in-
Charge of Tiljala P.S on every Monday of each
English Calendar Month till the disposal of appeal
and he shall surrender his passport, if any, till
disposal of bail I.d. to J.C. The Officer-in-Charge,
Tiljala P.S. is directed to submit report by the
next date fixed. 
If on bail to 24.06.2026 for appearance of appellant
and awaiting further order. 
Let the copy of this order be sent to the Officer-
in-Charge of Tiljala P.S. for the information and
taking necessary action.”
2. A perusal of the above order would indicate that one of the
conditions that has been imposed on the appellant is he should not
leave the jurisdiction of Tiljala P.S and to provide local address
to the Officer-in-Charge of Tiljala P.S.
3. Contending inter alia that the said condition is onerous and
the appellant claims to be in penury and is unable to pay high rent
for accommodation at Tiljala, where he is residing within the
jurisdiction of Tiljala P.S. namely paying a rent of Rs.8,500/- per
month, it is also contended that the appellant is permanent
resident of Village – VTC Padma Pukuria, PO – Mamabhafina, Sub
District:- Bagda, District – North 24-Parganas, West Bengal -
743232 and as such seeks alteration of the condition to permit him
to reside at his village. His prayer being genuine, we alter the
condition of residence and permit the appellant to reside at
Village – VTC Padma Pukuria, PO – Mamabhafina, Sub District:-
2

--- Page 321 ---
Bagda,  District  –  North  24-Parganas,  West  Bengal  –  743232.
Accordingly, I.A. stands allowed in part. It is made clear that the
other conditions which have been imposed by the trial court shall
remain unaltered. 
  (NEHA GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

SLP (CRL.) NO.12206/2026
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned
counsel for the respondent-State of Chhattisgarh, who has
appeared on advance notice.
2. The petitioner has been made an accused in connection
with FIR No. 201 of 2025, dated 05.08.2025, registered at
Police Station Jashpur, District Jashpur, Chhattisgarh
for the offence punishable under Sections 34(1)(a), 34(2)
and 42 of the Chhattisgarh Excise Act, which relates to
recovery of 15236 bottles of liquor, which were being
transported illegally without any proper authorization/
document. 
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
allegation is that the vehicle on which the consignment
was being transported was got refueled and payment for
the fuel was made by the petitioner. It was submitted
that the petitioner is in the business of transport and
at the request of one of his colleagues had arranged and
paid for the fuel. It was further submitted that the
maximum sentence which can be imposed under the Sections
which have been invoked in the present case is three
years and the petitioner is in custody for about eight
months. He further submitted that the petitioner has two
2

--- Page 324 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.12206/2026
antecedents, one under the Excise Act in the State of
Punjab, in which he is on bail; and the other under
Section 223 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, which has
been settled between the parties.
4. Learned  counsel  for  the  respondent-State  of
Chhattisgarh  submits  that  link  has  been  established
between the petitioner and the consignment recovered.
However,  she  also  submits  that  with  regard  to  the
antecedents, the submissions of learned counsel for the
petitioner are correct. 
5. Having  considered  the  matter,  the  petitioner  is
directed to be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as may be imposed by the Trial Court. Further,
the petitioner shall cooperate in the investigation/
trial. 
6. The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of in the
aforementioned terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

C.A. No.8793-8794/2026
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We heard Ms. Meenakshi Arora, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellant, Mr. Navin Pahwa, the learned senior
counsel appearing for the respondent no.1 (shareholder), Mr. Rajesh
Kumar Gautam, the learned senior counsel appearing for the Indian
Bank. We also heard Mr. Krishnan Venugopal, the learned senior
counsel appearing for the respondent no.2 and in the last, we also
heard Mr. C.A. Sundaram, the learned senior counsel appearing for a
party, namely, Brightlane Infraspace Pvt. Ltd., who has some
interest in this litigation.
2. In the appeal before us, the following reliefs have been
prayed for:-
“A. Admit and the Allow the instant Civil Appeal against
the impugned judgment and interim orders dated 17.06.2026
& 29.06.2026 in I.A. No.4230 of 2026 in Company Appeal
(AT) No.210 of 2026 passed by the Hon'ble National Company
Law Appellate Tribunal Principal Bench, New Delhi in the
interest of justice; and 
B. Permit Respondent No. 2 Company to raise capital by
way of a Rights Issue at par offered to all its existing
shareholders in proportion to their shareholding, in terms
of the proposal made before the Appellate Tribunal, the
Appellant and Respondent No. 5 being ready and willing to
contribute the entire amount including to the extent of
any shares remaining unsubscribed;
C. Pass any other order and/or directions, as this
Hon'ble Court may be fit and proper under the facts and
circumstances of the present case”
3. Having regard to the nature of the submissions canvassed
before us, it is difficult for us to adjudicate the disputed
2

--- Page 327 ---
C.A. No.8793-8794/2026
question of fact.
4. The matter is coming up before the NCLAT Principal Bench, New
Delhi on 23.07.2026. It shall be open for all the concerned to
raise all legal contentions available to them before the NCLAT on
23.07.2026.
5. It shall also be open for the client of Mr. Sundaram to make
his submissions before the NCLAT, if at all, he is a party before
the NCLAT. If the client of Mr. Sundaram is not a party, he may
apply and it is for the NCLAT to decide whether the client of Mr.
Sundaram is a proper or a necessary party in the proceedings.
6. We make an earnest request to the NCLAT to see that on
23.07.2026 the matter is heard for sure. We make this request to
the NCLAT because we are informed that NCLT has reserved the order
insofar Section 7 IBC application is concerned.
7. With the aforesaid, this appeal stands disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION   
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 3073 OF 2025
NEELAM RANI NEE DE                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
BHASKAR DE                                         Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. The petitioner-wife has filed the petition under Section 25 of
the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before this Court seeking
transfer of case bearing Matrimonial Suit No. 2002 of 2025 titled
as “Sri Bhaskar De vs. Smt. Neelam Rani Nee De” pending before the
Court of Addl. District and Sessions Judge, Fast Track Court-IV,
Barrakpore, North 24 Parganas, West Bengal to the Family Court at
District Jalandhar, Punjab.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
3. During the course of hearing, learned counsel for the parties
have agreed to transfer the aforesaid case  to the Court of
competent jurisdiction at Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi instead of
Family Court at District Jalandhar, Punjab.  We accordingly dispose
of the transfer petition and direct the transfer of case bearing
Matrimonial Suit No. 2002 of 2025 titled as “ Sri Bhaskar De  vs.
Smt. Neelam Rani Nee De” pending before the Court of Addl. District
1 of 3

--- Page 332 ---
and Sessions Judge, Fast Track Court-IV, Barrakpore, North 24
Parganas, West Bengal to the Court of competent jurisdiction at
Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi.
4. Let the record of the case be transferred without delay.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court for
compliance. 
6. If  video  conferencing  facility  is  available  with  the
transferee Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to the
parties.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
       …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
     …………………………………………………………………………J.

Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Kamlesh Kumar Maurya, Adv.
                   Ms. Priya Maurya, Adv.

                   Mrs. Priyanka Singh, Adv.

                   Mr. Shubham Kumar Singh, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Ms. Srishti Singh, AOR
                   Ms. Preetika Dwivedi, Adv.
                                      
         UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Petitioners  have  been  convicted
for  committing  offence  punishable  under
Sections 498A and 304B of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860 (for short, IPC’) and Section-4 of
the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961 (for short,
‘DP  Act’).  They  have  been  sentenced  to

--- Page 338 ---
2
undergo simple imprisonment for 02 years and
08 years respectively for committing offence
under  the  IPC  and  to  undergo  rigorous
imprisonment for 06 months for committing
offence under Section-4 of the DP Act.
2. Under the impugned order dated 07th
April,  2026  passed  by  the  High  Court,
petitioners’ prayer for suspension of their
sentence  is  rejected  and  hearing  of  the
appeal is expedited.
3. The  petitioners  have  already
suffered approximately three and a half years
of  incarceration.  Therefore,  if  the
petitioners’ appeal is not heard on merits by
the  High  Court  at  an  early  date,  the
petitioners  would  undergo  entire  sentence
imposed upon them.
4. Under  these  circumstances,  we
request the High Court to make all possible
endeavour to decide the petitioners’ pending
appeal within six months from today. If the
appeal is not decided within six months from
today,  this  Court  shall  consider  the
petitioners’ prayer for suspension of their
sentence.
5. Post the matter for consideration
in the month of February, 2027.

--- Page 339 ---
3
6. A copy of this order be sent to
the concerned High Court. Petitioners shall
also produce a copy of this order before the
concerned  High  Court  along  with  an
application for early hearing of the pending
appeal.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

the polling scheduled for 19 th July, 2026. However,
the rights and obligations of the petitioner as a
voter shall remain subject to the final outcome of
this petition.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.319  OF 2026
THONGMINTHANG HAOKIP @
THANGBOI HAOKIP @ ROGER       PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
NATIONAL INVESTIGATION
AGENCY, IMPHAL              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This transfer petition has been filed by the
petitioner seeking transfer of case bearing Special
Trial (NIA) Case No.01 of 2025 titled as  ‘National
Investigation Agencey (NIA) vs. Thongminthang Haolip
@ Thangboi Haokip @ Roger’  pending before the Ld.
Special Court-1, NIA, Manipur at Imphal to competent
Court in Delhi.
2. Ms.  Aishwarya  Bhati,  learned  Additional
Solicitor General, appearing for the respondent-
National  Investigation  Agency  has  pointed  out
paragraph 19(e) of the Counter Affidavit that the
Special  Court,  i.e.,  NIA  Special  Court  III  in
Churachandpur, Manipur, has been set up and is
functioning since 22.05.2025 and in that Court the
case could be tried.
1

--- Page 350 ---
3. Mr.  Colin  Gonsalves,  learned  senior  counsel
appearing  for  the  petitioner,  submits  that  the
petitioner has no objection in case the matter (Case
No.01 of 2025) is transferred to the aforesaid newly
established Court.
4. Accordingly, we direct that the case bearing
Special Trial (NIA) Case No.01 of 2025 titled as
‘National  Investigation  Agencey  (NIA)  vs.
Thongminthang Haolip @ Thangboi Haokip @ Roger’   be
transferred  to  the  NIA  Special  Court  III  in
Churachandpur, Manipur.
5. Records  of  the  case  should  immediately  be
transferred to the transferee court.
6. The Transfer Petition is allowed accordingly.
7. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
   (SANDEEP MEHTA)

JUDGMENT
 
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s): Mr. Arvind Kumar Sharma, AOR
                   
                   Mrs.Aishwarya Bhati, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Vikramjit Banerjee, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Abhishek Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Advitiya Awasthi, Adv.
                   Mr. S.A.Haseeb, Adv.
                   Mr. Ameyavikrma Thanvi, Adv.
                   Mr. Shreya Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Sachin Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
                   Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AOR
Mr. Sunit Choudhary, Adv.
Ms. Shreya Jain, ADv.
                   
For Respondent(s): 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The  respondent-Society  was  served  on  13.05.2025.
However, there is no appearance on behalf of the said
entity.
In  the  circumstances,  we  have  heard  learned
1

--- Page 353 ---
Additional Solicitor General (ASG) for the petitioner.
By  intimation  dated  15.04.2022,  the  renewal
application made by the respondent-Society under Section
16(1) read with Section 12(4)(f)(vi) and Section 12(4)(f)
(vi) of the Foreign Contribution Regulation Act, 2010 (for
short, “the Act”) was declined to the respondent-Society.
The said intimation was assailed before the High Court in
WP (C) No.17524 of 2022.
By  the  impugned  order,  dated  12.09.2023,  the
Division Bench of the High Court set aside the said
intimation and allowed the Writ Petition.
Being aggrieved, the Union of India has preferred
this Special Leave Petition.
We  have  heard  learned  ASG  appearing  for  the
petitioner. As noted above, there is no appearance on
behalf of the respondent-Society.
Having heard learned ASG for the petitioner, we find
that the interest of justice would be served in the
instant case by setting aside the impugned order and
reserving liberty to the respondent-Society to make a
fresh  application  seeking  permission  for  grant  of
registration under the provisions of the aforesaid Act. 
It  is  needless  to  observe  that  if  such  an
application for grant of registration is made by the
respondent-Society, the same shall be considered on its
own  merits  and  de  hors  the  earlier  refusal  dated
15.04.2022 which was also set aside by the Division Bench
2

--- Page 354 ---
of the High Court and in accordance with law. 
If such an application is made by the respondent-
Society,  the  petitioner  herein  shall  make  a  fresh
inspection and prepare a fresh report.
In the circumstances, having granted the aforesaid
liberty, we set aside the impugned order.
The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
aforesaid terms. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(B. LAKSHMI MANIKYA VALLI)                      (DIVYA BABBAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Criminal Appeal No. 2982/2026 
1. Heard Mr. Anuvrat Sharma, learned counsel for
the appellant and Ms. Pallavi Langar, learned counsel
for the State.

--- Page 367 ---
2
2. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that
the appellant has already undergone about 15 years and
03 months as on February, 2026.
3. Considering  the  period  undergone  by  the
appellant, we are inclined to enlarge the appellant on
bail pending disposal of the appeal before this Court.
4. Accordingly, pending disposal of the appeal,
the appellant- Bickey Lohar shall be enlarged on bail
subject to the satisfaction of the concerned Trial

                   Mr. Rajat Vaishnaw, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard Mr. Raghavendra P.Shankar, learned
Additional  Solicitor  General  appearing  for  the
petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Learned Additional Solicitor General submits that
the High Court erred in treating the case as of low tax
effect case.
1

--- Page 389 ---
4. To satisfy ourselves, we examined the order of the
Assessment Officer as well as the order of the Income Tax
Appellate  Tribunal.   On  the  special  facts,  having
examined the case on merits, we are convinced that the
present  case does  not call  for any  interference in
exercise of our jurisdiction under Article 136 of the
Constitution of India.  The Special Leave Petition is
accordingly dismissed.
5. Any question of law on the aspect of interpretation
of  clause  3.1  (h)  of  the  Circular  No.5/2024
F.No.279/Misc.142/2007-ITJ(Pt.)  dated  15th March,  2024
with regard to the case being of low tax effect or not is
kept open.

disposed of.

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The special leave petition is directed against an order dated 27 th April,
2026 whereby a learned Judge of the High Court of Bombay, seized of Notice of
Motion No.763 of 2019 1 and connected applications, recused from hearing the
same upon an objection raised by the defendant no.1-HDFC Bank Limited.
1 Notice of Motion
1

--- Page 407 ---
2. Mr.  Oza,  learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner  may  be
justified in his grievance that the notice of motion is yet to be decided and
taken to its logical conclusion despite the same having been listed before six
different Single Judges; however, having regard to the nature of the order which
is impugned, we hold the special leave petition not to be maintainable.
3. We are informed by Mr. Oza that the learned Judge 2, who is now seized of
the Notice of Motion, has fixed 27 th July, 2026 (at 3:30 PM) as the next date of
hearing thereof. 
4. In view  of  the  same, we  dispose  of  the  special leave  petition with  a
request to the said learned Judge to make sincere endeavour to decide the
Notice of Motion upon granting opportunity of hearing to all concerned parties
at the earliest, and preferably by the year end subject, of course, to the Court’s
convenience. 
5. All points and contentions on merits are kept open.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
      (MANIK KUMAR)                         (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)
2 Justice Abhay Ahuja

learned senior counsel appearing for respondent No.6.
1

--- Page 443 ---
2. Learned senior counsel for the petitioner submits
that pursuant to the order of this Court dated 8 th April,
2026,  the process  has started  for appointment  of a
regular Principal.  Learned senior counsel also submits
that pursuant to the order of the District Inspector of
School,  holding  the  penalty  of  dismissal  to  be
disproportionate,  a  decision  has  been  taken  by  the
petitioner-Committee of Management on 22 nd December, 2025
relegating the respondent No.6 to the post of Lecturer
(Sociology).  Learned senior counsel also submits that by
the order dated 28 th April, 2026 the order relegating
respondent  No.6  as  Lecturer  (Sociology)  has  been
confirmed by the District Inspector of School.
3. Learned senior counsel for respondent No.6 disputes
this position.  Learned senior counsel wants to file a
rejoinder to the affidavit of compliance filed by the
petitioner and seeks time for the same.
4. Time prayed for is granted.
5. Let the necessary response to the affidavit of
compliance be filed on or before 1st August, 2026.
6. List the matter on 31st August, 2026.
7. In the meantime, the process for appointment of the
regular  Principal,  as  ordered  by  this  Court,  shall
continue.

                   Mr. Dhananjya Kumar Tyagi, Adv.
                   Dr. Sushil Balwada, AOR 
  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. An Interlocutory Application bearing Interlocutory 
Application No.154384/2026 has been filed on behalf of  
the de-facto complainant/informant seeking impleadment  
as respondent in the present case. The same is allowed  
and the de-facto complainant/informant is impleaded as  
1

--- Page 481 ---
Respondent No.2 in the present case.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner,  
learned counsel for Respondent No.1-State of Uttar  
Pradesh  and  learned  counsel  for  Respondent  No.2-
informant.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that  
petitioner is in custody since 02.12.2024. In the  
trial, all material witnesses have been examined.
4. Learned counsel for Respondent No.1-State of Uttar 
Pradesh submits that it is a case of gruesome murder  
with the role of the petitioner clearly discernible.  
Similar sentiment has been expressed by learned counsel 
for Respondent No.2.
5. In the course of the hearing, it has come to light 
that evidence of 10 prosecution witnesses have been  
recorded. On a query by the court, learned counsel for  
Respondent No.1-State of Uttar Pradesh submits that all 
material witnesses have been examined.
6. Before we pass a final order, one way or the  
other, we direct learned counsel for the petitioner to  
place on record the deposition of all the material  
witnesses.
7. For this purpose, two weeks’ time is granted.
8. List the matter on 31.07.2026.

ITEM NO.3               COURT NO.15               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No. 10361/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 18-02-2026
in IA No. 1/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna]
BIRBAHADUR SINGH @SIKRI SINGH                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 167991/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Gyanendra Kumar, Adv.
                    Mr. Yashasvi Virendra, AOR
                    Mr. Shantanu Sagar, Adv.
                                       
For Respondent(s) : Ms. Rebecca Mishra, Adv.
                    Mr. Azmat Hayat Amanullah, AOR
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner as well as
learned counsel for the respondent-State. We have also perused the
evidence of the Doctor (PW-7) and that of the injured complainant
(PW-1).
2. Though the injuries  inflicted by  the firearm used by the
petitioner are simple in nature, that by itself may not be a ground
to suspend the sentence of life imprisonment, more so when the
petitioner has not yet completed three years of incarceration.
1

--- Page 485 ---
3. While we do not agree with the view taken by the High Court
that the petitioner can renew his prayer for bail once he completes
five years of incarceration as there is no such hard and fast rule,
we are however, of the view that having regard to the fact that the
appeal  pending  before  the  High  Court  is  of  the  year  2023,
petitioner can renew his prayer for suspension of sentence if the
appeal is not heard within the next six months.
4. Subject to the observation made above, the Special Leave
Petition is dismissed.
5.  Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
  (IRAMNAZ)                                   (CHETNA BALOONI)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  10526/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 27-02-2026 in
CRLMBA No. 7328/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
AMIT TYAGI                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P                                       Respondent(s)
(IA No. 169550/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT and IA No. 169552/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Anuroop Chakravarti, Adv.
                   Mr. M.S. Vishnu Sankar, Adv.
                   Ms. Vishakha Pandae, Adv.
                   Mr. Yashaswin Venugopal Bajpai, Adv.
                   Mr. Aryan Shankar, Adv.
                   M/s Lawfic, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) :Dr. Vijendra Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Kumar Abhinandan, Adv.
                   Ms. Sneh Suman, Adv.
                   Ms. Kim Rani, Adv.                              
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Although the instant Special Leave Petition is purportedly
against an innocuous order dated 27.02.2026, whereby the third bail
application of the petitioner was directed to be listed before an
appropriate Bench, in sum and substance, the prayer sought is for
expeditious disposal of the said bail application, which is pending
before the High Court. It may be true that the petitioner is
1

--- Page 489 ---
involved in 21 cases, but the fact remains that he has already
spent 5 years in custody.
2. In light of the principles laid down by this Court in a catena
of judgments, it seems to us that High Court needs to decide the
pending bail application in a time-bound manner. Consequently, we
dispose of this Special Leave Petition with a request to the High
Court to decide the pending bail application expeditiously and
preferably within two weeks.
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand closed.
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
2

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Special Leave Petition (C) No.17119/2025:-
1. The aforesaid Special Leave Petition was listed today as
Mentioning Item No.801 seeking withdrawal of the said petition.
2. The  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner  has
instructions from his client not to press this petition and
withdraw the same as her client wants to avail the remedy under the
West Bengal Sales Tax (Settlement of Dispute) Act, 1999 as amended
by the West Bengal Sales Tax (Settlement of Dispute) (Amendment)
Act, 2025.

--- Page 493 ---
2
3. We permit the petitioner to go before the competent authority
under the Amendment Act of 2025 and avail the appropriate relief.
4. If the petitioner applies with the competent authority of the
State under the Amendment Act, 2025, the State shall allow the
application  in  terms  of  the  Amendment  Act  and  accept  the
application filed by the petitioner.
5. In view of the aforesaid, the petition stand dismissed as not
pressed.
Special Leave Petition(C) No. 15823/2025:-
1. The aforesaid Special Leave Petition was also listed today as
Mentioning Item No.801 seeking withdrawal of the said petition.
2. The learned counsel appearing for the petitioner submits that
subsequent to the filing of the Special Leave Petition, the State
Government  has  issued  Notification  dated  16.07.2025  thereby
introducing the West Bengal Sales Tax (Settlement of Dispute)
(Amendment) Act, 2025 which amended the West Bengal Sales Tax
(Settlement of Dispute) Act, 1999, whereunder an opportunity has
been granted for settlement of claims of entry tax levied under the
West Bengal Tax on Entry of Goods into Local Areas Act, 2012.
3. The learned counsel further submits that the petitioner has
already availed the benefit of the provisions of the West Bengal
Sales Tax (Settlement of Dispute) Act, 1999, as amended by the West
Bengal Sales Tax (Settlement of Dispute) (Amendment) Act, 2025, and
the dispute forming the subject matter of the present Petition has
been amicably and voluntarily settled in the Pre-Special Lok Adalat
Conciliation Meeting held on 15.06.2026.
4. In view of the said settlement, the learned counsel for the
petitioner does not wish to proceed with the present Petition and
seeks permission of this Court to withdraw the same as having
become infructuous.
5. With  the  aforesaid,  this  petition  stands  dismissed  as
withdrawn.

DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

impugned order, we do not find it to be a fit case where we

should interfere inasmuch as the order which is impugned is

aforesaid liberty, this petition is disposed of.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.20599/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 15-09-2025
in CRM No.51783/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna]
PRAKASH BADAL                                      Petitioner
                                VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.                              Respondents
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHEEL NAGU
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Varun Thakur, Adv.
                   Mr. Ramkaran, Adv.
                   Ms. Shraddha Saran, Adv.
                   Mr. Tanuj Bagga Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Deepak Goel, Adv.
                   Mr. S. P. Singh, Adv.
                   M/S. Varun Thakur & Associates, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Anshul Narayan, Addl. Standing Counsel, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashutosh Chaturvedi, Adv.
                   Mr. Anshuman Harsh, Adv.
                   Mr. Prem Prakash, AOR
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have read the report of the Senior Superintendent of Police,
Patna dated 14th July, 2026.
2. Respondent no.2, who has been granted bail by the order dated 15 th
September, 2025 of the High Court, has been evading arrest.
3. Raids  conducted  by  the  Bihar  Police  have  revealed  that  the
1

--- Page 517 ---
respondent no.2 has possibly fled to Jharkhand.
4. However, from the aforesaid report of the Senior Superintendent of
Police we do not find any effort having been made by the Bihar Police to
coordinate  with  the  Jharkhand  Police  to  trace  the  whereabouts  of  the
respondent no.2.
5. In view thereof, we adjourn hearing of the special leave petition for a
period of three weeks from date.
6. Relist the special leave petition on 12th August, 2026. 
7. In  the  meanwhile,  the  Bihar  Police  shall  not  leave  any  stone
unturned  to track the respondent  no.2 and to give  effect to the  non-
bailable warrant of arrest issued in terms of the order dated 21 st May,
2026 of this Court.
  (RASHMI DHYANI PANT)                       (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
 ASST. REGISTRAR-CUM-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
 
2

with remission is 16 years 3 months and 3 days.
2. Hence, the sentence imposed on the petitioner in SLP (Crl.)
No.16945/2025, is suspended and he is ordered to be released on
bail subject to such terms and conditions as the jurisdictional
court may deem fit to impose.
3. In the meanwhile, the petitioner in SLP (Crl.) No.16945/2025,
shall apply for remission by filing appropriate application before
the competent authority within two weeks from today and in the
event of such application being filed, the competent authority
shall consider the same expeditiously and at any rate within an
outer limit of three months.
4. List after three months.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).19310/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 13-10-2023
in CRLA No.1352/2015 passed by the High Court of Chhatisgarh at
Bilaspur]
RAVINDRA PRASAD @ LAVKUSH SAH                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                              Respondent(s)
(IA No. 127437/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Sangeeta Kumar, AoR
                   Mrs. Vidushi Garg, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mrs. Prerna Dhall, Adv.
                   Mr. Ambuj Swaroop, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashi Kant, Adv.
                   Mr. Avinash Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Roshni Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Prashant Singh, AoR
                                      
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned counsel
for the respondent-State of Chhattisgarh.
2. The petitioner seeks interference in the impugned judgment
dated 13.10.2023 passed by the High Court of Chhattisgarh at
Bilaspur  in  Criminal  Appeal  No.1352  of  2015,  by  which  the

--- Page 555 ---
2
conviction and sentence as awarded to the petitioner by the Trial
Court in Sessions Case No.163 of 2013 vide order dated 30.04.2015
has been upheld.
3. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through
the material on record, we find that no ground for interference by
this Court is made out. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition
stands dismissed.
4. However, in view of the fact that the petitioner has already
undergone thirteen years and three months of actual incarceration,
the authorities concerned are required to consider the case of the
petitioner for premature release under the existing policy of the
State Government. The said consideration be done promptly on the
date the petitioner becomes eligible for such consideration. Final
decision be taken by the Competent Authority and order passed
within two months from the date of initiation of the process for
premature release of the petitioner. 
5. We  only  indicate  that  even  otherwise,  the  authorities
concerned are required to suo motu initiate the case of any convict
for premature release  in terms of  the directions given by a
Coordinate Bench of this Court in order dated 18.02.2025 in Suo
Motu Writ Petition (Crl.) No.4 of 2021.
6. Learned counsel for the respondent-State of Chhattisgarh shall
ensure compliance of the same.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

      UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The counter affidavit filed by the State of Punjab discloses
that the petitioner, Sarwan Singh, failed to give details of all
the criminal cases registered against him in the past.

vs. State of Uttar Pradesh”1, the failure to disclose these details
12025 SCC OnLine SC 1319

--- Page 565 ---
is fatal to the petitioner’s case.

short ground.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following

Coordinate Bench of this Court vide order dated 29.05.2026 on the
terms indicated therein which reads as under:
“a) The trial is expedited. We request the Trial Court to
preferably conclude the trial within a period of one (01)
year from today; 
b) The prosecution undertakes to produce such of those
witnesses who need to be examined, on all dates, as may
be fixed by the Trial Court; and 
c) The parties undertake to fully cooperate and not seek
any unnecessary adjournment.”
3. In view of the same, we do not find any good ground to take a
different view than the one taken in the aforesaid special leave
petition. Accordingly, the special leave petitions stand disposed
of.
4. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

For Respondent(s)

                             O R D E R
I.A. Nos. 54100/2024, 54103/2024,   71551/2026 and 71552/2026  
These applications were filed in relation to the deceased
petitioner.  The application filed for condonation of delay in the
filing of an application for substitution is misconceived as the
matter stood abated and, thereafter, there was delay in seeking the
1

--- Page 577 ---
setting aside of the abatement. In relation to the same, a separate
application was filed for condonation of delay.
The other applications are ordered, condoning the delay in
seeking setting aside of the abatement; setting aside the said
abatement; and bringing the legal representatives of the deceased
petitioner on record. 
The cause title shall be amended accordingly.
I.A. No. 18156/2022
Insofar as the deceased respondent is concerned, the present
application was filed seeking to bring his legal representatives on
record. 
We, however, find that no death certificate has been filed. 
Learned counsel for the petitioner shall ascertain the date of
death and take necessary steps accordingly before the next date of
hearing. 
Re-list the matter on 18.08.2026. The matter shall retain its
position in the list on the next date.

ITEM NO.8               COURT NO.9               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No. 12125/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 05-06-2026
in CRLMBA No. 14507/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
TAUQEER RAZA KHAN                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P                                       Respondent(s)
(IA No. 190053/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT and IA No. 190054/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.) 
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJEEV SACHDEVA
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Ananad Padmanabhan, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Asad Alvi, Adv.
                   Mr. Hamd Irfan, Adv.
                   Ms. Saba Asad, Adv.
                   Mr. Satya Kam Sharma, AOR       
                               
For Respondent(s) Mr. S.V. Raju, ASG
Mr. Sharan Dev Singh Thakur, AAG
Ms. Sakshi Kakkar, Adv. 
Mr. Samrat, Adv. 
Ms. Divya Prakash Angi, Adv. 
Mr. Shivansh Singh, Adv. 
     UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The present petition was filed for grant of regular bail in
relation to First Information Report No. 1146/2025, registered with
Police Station - Baradari, District – Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh, for
offences punishable under Sections 190, 191(2), 191(3), 124(2),
121, 125, 352, 351(3), 109, 299, 223, 61(2) of Bharatiya Nyaya
1

--- Page 581 ---
Sanhita, 2023 and Section 7 of Criminal Law Amendment Act.  
However, the learned Additional Solicitor General appearing
for the State of U.P., asserted that the petitioner, Tauqeer Raza
Khan, failed to disclose all the criminal cases registered against
him in the past.  A list of 24 such cases was furnished by him but
only 12 of those cases have been mentioned in the synopsis of the
special leave petition. 
In the light of the law laid down by this Court in “Munnesh
vs. State of Uttar Pradesh”1, failure on the part of the petitioner
to disclose his past criminal antecedents is fatal. 
The special leave petition is liable to be dismissed on this
short ground and is, accordingly, dismissed. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
 (BABITA PANDEY)                                (PREETI SAXENA)
    AR-cum-PS                           COURT MASTER (NSH)
1 2025 SCC OnLine SC 1319
2

--- Page 582 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R

applicants who contends that the applicants had no intention of
misleading the Court, and petitioners being in penury, the payment
of costs, as ordered by this Court, would be onerous and prays for

--- Page 585 ---
waiving of the costs.

counsel, we are persuaded to reduce the costs to Rs.12,000/-

Lakhs only) as ordered and the said amount of Rs.12,000/- shall be
deposited by the applicants as ordered in paragraph 5 of the order
dated 28.05.2025 within six (6) weeks.
4. Accordingly, Miscellaneous Application stands disposed of.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  13-03-2026
in WA No. 200085/2026 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Kalaburagi]
SYD ASHRAF RAZA & ANR.                             Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SYED SURRIYA TANVEER & ORS.                        Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Hma Rahman, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohd. Ashraf Mansoori, Adv.
                   Ms. Kumari Rashmi Rani, Adv.
                   Ms. Rashi Jaiswal, Adv.
                   Mr. Narender Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashish Pandey, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioners seeks a week’s time on the
ground of ill health of the senior counsel. We notice that against
an order of injunction granted by the tribunal, the petition under
Section 227 of the Constitution of India has been filed, which is
prima facie not maintenable in view of Order XLIII Rule 1(r) of the
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), which undisputedly governs
such cases.

--- Page 597 ---
2. In fact, this Court in the case of Virudhunagar Hindu Nadargal
Dharma Paribalana Sabai and Others v. Tuticorin Educational Society
and Others, (2019) 9 SCC 538, has held that parties should not be
permitted to bypass the remedial provisions available under law.
3. At the request of learned counsel for the petitioner, list next
week.
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

               RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL  LEAVE  PETITION  (CRIMINAL)  Diary
No(s).39328/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order
dated 08-05-2025 in CRLA No.799/2015 passed by the
High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad]
RAMILABEN                            Petitioner(s)
                       VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT                     Respondent(s)
IA No. 189956/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN 
FILING
IA No. 189955/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF 
THE IMPUGNED JUDGMENT 
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Aastha Mehta, Adv.
                   Ms. Prerana Mohapatra, Adv.
                   Ms. Prina Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Anshuman Srivastava, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
Annu, Adv. 
    UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the 
following
                      O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Issue notice.
3. Ms.  Swati  Ghildiyal,  learned  AOR  accepts
notice on behalf of the respondent – State.
1

--- Page 609 ---
4. Having heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties and on perusal of the records, it
would indicate that petitioner has been in custody
for the past one year and lodged in Vadodara
Central Jail, Gujarat. Having regard to the fact
that the daughter of the petitioner and also
accused No.1 having been granted bail by this
court and against the daughter, the allegation as
made by the prosecution against the petitioner is
similar, we see no reason to take a different view
and  on  the  ground  of  parity,  namely,  the
allegation made by the prosecution against the
petitioner (mother) and the co-accused – daughter
being one and the same, we suspend the sentence
and order the petitioner to be enlarged on bail on
such terms and conditions as the jurisdictional
Court may impose.
5. Post this matter alongwith SLP (Crl.) Diary
No.64523/2026.
  (NEHA GUPTA)                     (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)               COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

Date : 15-07-2026 This appeal was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Appellant(s) : 
                   Mr. Adolf Mathew, Adv.
                   Ms. Meenu George, Adv.
                   Mr. Shishir Pinaki, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This is one of those very unfortunate matters where the
petitioner - father is alleged to have sexually assaulted his
own daughter. 
2. The petitioner was put to trial in the Court of the
Sessions Judge at Alappuzha in Sessions Case No. 1145 of 2010
for the offence punishable under Section 376 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 (for short, “IPC”).
3. At the end of the trial, the petitioner was held guilty
of the alleged crime and was sentenced to undergo life
imprisonment with a fine of 5,000/-.₹
4. The petitioner went in appeal before the High Court. The
appeal also failed. In such circumstances, the petitioner is
here before us with the present petition.

--- Page 619 ---
2
5. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner - convict
is undergoing sentence past 16 years. 
6. We are informed that the State of Kerala has no Remission
Policy having regard to the nature of the crime alleged to
have been committed. 
7. We are further informed that the victim is now a married
girl. She is well settled. We are further informed that the
mother of the victim is also residing along with her daughter
at her matrimonial home. 
8. Before we proceed to pass any further order, we want the
State  to  provide  us  with  some  report  as  regards  the
whereabouts and status of the victim and the mother of the
victim.  The inquiry shall be carried out very discreetly. 
9. Let such a Report come on record. We would also like to
know from the jail authorities the overall conduct of the
petitioner in the last 14 years. 
10. Post this matter next week on top of the board. 
  (VISHAL ANAND)                                   (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned Senior Counsel for the parties.
2. The High Court, vide impugned order, has merely directed a
fresh audit of the accounts of the petitioner–Association. Once the
audit is complete and report is prepared, let such report be placed
before the High Court in the writ proceedings, which have been
disposed of vide the impugned order. The High Court is requested to
1

--- Page 663 ---
revive the proceedings for the limited purpose of hearing both
sides on the audit report that would be submitted in compliance
with the High Court directions.
3. The petitioner shall be at liberty to urge that there was
already a previous audit report, prepared strictly in accordance
with Section 12(C) and 12(D) of the Societies Registration Act,
1860. The consequential action, pursuant to the fresh audit report,
if any, shall be directed only after hearing both sides.
4. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.
5. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
